docs.title: Home Application
docs.text:
Welcome to the Home Application, a centralized platform designed to empower families with collaborative household management tools. Built with a focus on privacy, real-time synchronization, and professional engineering standards.

  • Collaborative Shopping

    Shared lists, intelligent price suggestions, and digital loyalty asset management.

  • Family Hierarchy

    Role-based permissions and automated age-group classification for household members.

  • Google-Powered Security

    Secure authentication via Google OAuth2 with a strict Zero-Trust API architecture.

  • Modern SPA

    A blazing-fast, mobile-first React experience optimized for in-store usage.

docs.title: Technical Ecosystem
docs.text:
The project is architected as a Gradle-managed monorepo, ensuring atomic changes and high maintainability.

Backend Frontend Verification Component Technology Framework Spring Boot 4.0 Language Java 25 Database PostgreSQL 17 API Pattern REST + HATEOAS (HAL) Migrations Liquibase Component Technology Library React 19 UI System Mantine 7 State TanStack Query v5 Build Vite 6 Types TypeScript 5 Layer Framework Integration Spock (Groovy) Containers Testcontainers E2E Playwright Mocks MSW / WireMock

docs.title: Project Documentation
docs.text:
Explore the comprehensive documentation to understand the "Why", "How", and "What" of the application.

  • User Guide

    Environment setup, feature tutorials, and troubleshooting for users and developers.

  • Requirements

    The definitive source of truth using EARS syntax and traceable User Journeys.

  • IconPencil Technical Design

    Detailed architecture, ER diagrams, and frontend component patterns.

  • API Reference

    Complete REST contract with interactive testing via ReDoc integration.

docs.title: Engineering Methodology
docs.text:
Spec-Driven Development (SDD)

This project strictly follows the SDD philosophy. We formalize requirements and architectural design before implementation, ensuring every feature is transactional, verifiable, and directly linked to a business need.

Learn more about our SDD Workflow

docs.title: Knowledge Base Overview
docs.text:
The Development Guidelines section serves as the centralized knowledge base for the Home Application ecosystem. It defines the technical standards, architectural patterns, and coding conventions that ensure consistency across the entire project. This repository of knowledge is designed to guide human developers and provide high-signal context for AI agents, establishing a clear "Source of Truth" for how logic should be reasoned, produced, and maintained.

By formalizing these patterns, we enable a highly autonomous development workflow where AI agents can learn the specific idiomatic styles of this codebase. These guidelines cover everything from backend service structures and database interactions to frontend component design and BDD-style testing. Adhering to these standards is mandatory for ensuring that every contribution remains scalable, secure, and perfectly aligned with the project's long-term architectural goals.

docs.title: Core Guidelines
docs.text:
• Java Guidelines

    Standards for Spring Boot 4.0, JPA entities, HATEOAS controllers, and service-layer patterns.

  • Spock Tests

    Guidelines for writing expressive BDD tests using Groovy, Testcontainers, and Mocking strategies.

  • :simple-playwright: Frontend E2E Tests

    Playwright E2E with Page Object Model and API mocking.

  • Documentation Guidelines

    Standards for MkDocs, visual patterns, and cross-linking rules.

  • SDD Methodology

    The Spec-Driven Development way of working, EARS syntax, and persistent anchors.

  • Frontend Guidelines

    React 19 conventions, Mantine UI usage, TanStack Query, and component architecture.

  • Database Guidelines

    PostgreSQL schemas, Liquibase migrations, naming conventions, and data integrity patterns.

docs.title: AI Alignment
docs.text:
For AI Agents

When operating within this workspace, AI agents MUST prioritize the instructions found in these guidelines over general default behaviors. These documents contain project-specific "Rules of Engagement" that are foundational to the technical integrity of the Home Application.

docs.title: Agent Context & Tooling
docs.text:
To ensure efficient operation and minimize context window saturation, the following resources are provided:

  • Project Context (.ai/project-context.md): Provides a high-level, summarized overview of the system architecture and module locations. It serves as a directory for the agent to find detailed information without overloading the initial session context.
  • Specialized Skills (.ai/skills/): Contains structured workflows for common operations such as committing code, generating Spock tests, and updating documentation. Agents should activate these skills when performing relevant tasks.
  • Kiro-CLI Integration (.kiro/): Defines a pre-configured agent that leverages these development guidelines as a RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) knowledge base. This allows for highly efficient retrieval of project directives and patterns.
  • Agent Mandates (GEMINI.md, CLAUDE.md): Platform-specific foundational instructions that assume a similar role to the Kiro agent configuration, ensuring that all AI assistants adhere to the core engineering standards of the project.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This document defines the standards for the PostgreSQL 17 database, including schema design, naming conventions, migration rules, and data integrity patterns. All schema changes are managed by Liquibase — Hibernate is configured with ddl-auto: validate and never modifies the schema.

docs.title: 1. Schema Architecture
docs.text:
The database is organized into two logical schemas that enforce domain boundaries:

Schema Purpose Examples profiles Identity, authentication, family structure user, user_profile, family_roles, age_group_config shopping Collaborative shopping experience shopping_lists, shopping_items, shopping_stores, coupons

Cross-Schema References

Cross-schema foreign keys are allowed for domain relationships (e.g., shopping.shopping_lists.created_by → profiles.user.id). Keep these minimal — each schema should be as self-contained as possible.

docs.title: File Structure
docs.text:
src/main/resources/db/changelog/
├── db.changelog-master.xml          # Master changelog (includeAll)
└── sql/
    ├── 01-profiles-schema.sql       # Profiles DDL + seed data
    ├── 02-shopping-schema.sql       # Shopping DDL
    └── 03-seed-data.sql             # Reference data

The master changelog uses includeAll to load all SQL files in alphabetical order:

<includeAll path="db/changelog/sql/" relativeToChangelogFile="false"/>

docs.title: Changeset Format
docs.text:
Use --liquibase formatted sql with --changeset author:id tags.

--liquibase formatted sql

--changeset jorge:04-add-shopping-tags
CREATE TABLE shopping.tags (
    id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY,
    name VARCHAR(50) NOT NULL UNIQUE,
    created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    updated_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
    version BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
);

docs.title: File Naming
docs.text:
New migration files follow the pattern <NN>-<description>.sql where NN is the next sequential number.

Changesets Are Immutable

Once a changeset has been applied to any environment, it must never be modified. Liquibase tracks changesets by checksum — modifying an applied changeset causes a checksum mismatch and breaks deployment. Always add new changesets for schema changes.

docs.title: Backward Compatibility
docs.text:
Migrations must be backward-compatible with the currently running application:

Safe Migration Rules

  • Add new columns as NULL or with a DEFAULT value.
  • Add new tables freely.
  • Add new indexes freely.
  • NEVER add NOT NULL to an existing column without a default.
  • NEVER rename a column in a single step — use add → migrate → drop across releases.
  • NEVER drop a column that the running application still reads.

docs.title: 3. Naming Conventions
docs.text:
All identifiers use snake_case.

Element Convention Example Schema Lowercase domain name profiles, shopping Table Plural noun, schema-qualified shopping.shopping_lists Column Descriptive snake_case created_by, family_role_id, due_date Primary Key id (always) id BIGSERIAL PRIMARY KEY Foreign Key fk_<table>_<referenced> fk_list_items_list Index idx_<table>_<columns> idx_user_email Unique Index idx_<table>_<columns> or inline UNIQUE idx_shopping_items_name_category Unique Constraint Inline UNIQUE on column email VARCHAR(100) NOT NULL UNIQUE

docs.title: 4. Data Types
docs.text:
Use Case Type Notes Primary keys BIGSERIAL or BIGINT GENERATED BY DEFAULT AS IDENTITY Auto-incrementing Foreign keys BIGINT Matches referenced PK type Short text VARCHAR(n) Length must match backend validation (e.g., email VARCHAR(100)) Long text TEXT Photos (base64), descriptions, markdown Money DECIMAL(10,2) Never use FLOAT for monetary values Booleans BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT <value> Always provide a default Timestamps TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP For audit columns Dates DATE For date-only values (e.g., birthdate) Counters/versions BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0 or INTEGER DEFAULT 0 For optimistic locking

VARCHAR Lengths Must Match Backend

Column lengths must align with Jakarta validation annotations on the corresponding DTO. For example, email VARCHAR(100) matches @Size(max = 100) on UserProfileDTO.email.

docs.title: 5. Mandatory Audit Columns
docs.text:
Every table MUST include the following columns:

created_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
updated_at TIMESTAMP NOT NULL DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP,
version BIGINT NOT NULL DEFAULT 0
Column Purpose created_at Immutable record creation timestamp updated_at Updated on every modification (managed by Hibernate @UpdateTimestamp) version Optimistic locking — prevents lost updates during concurrent edits

Exception: Append-Only Tables

History/log tables (e.g., shopping_item_price_history) use recorded_at instead and omit updated_at/version since rows are never modified.

docs.title: 6. Indexing Strategy
docs.text:
When to Add Indexes

  • Unique business keys — UNIQUE on natural identifiers (email, name).
  • Foreign key columns — always index FK columns for join performance.
  • Frequent query filters — index columns used in WHERE clauses (e.g., status, used).
  • Composite indexes — for multi-column queries, order columns by selectivity.
  • AVOID indexing low-cardinality boolean columns alone — combine with other columns (e.g., idx_coupons_status_due(used, due_date)).
-- Unique business key
CREATE INDEX idx_user_email ON profiles.user(email);

-- Composite index for query optimization (most selective first, DESC for recent-first)
CREATE INDEX idx_price_history_item_store
    ON shopping.shopping_item_price_history(item_id, store_id, recorded_at DESC);

-- Combined filter index for dashboard widget
CREATE INDEX idx_coupons_status_due ON shopping.coupons(used, due_date);

docs.title: 7. Foreign Key Patterns
docs.text:
Strategy Use When Example ON DELETE CASCADE Child has no meaning without parent shopping_list_items → shopping_lists ON DELETE SET NULL Reference is optional, child survives shopping_list_items.store_id → shopping_stores No action (RESTRICT) Deletion should be blocked shopping_lists.created_by → profiles.user 
-- CASCADE: items are deleted with their list
CONSTRAINT fk_list_items_list FOREIGN KEY (list_id)
    REFERENCES shopping.shopping_lists(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,

-- SET NULL: item keeps its record but loses store reference
CONSTRAINT fk_list_items_store FOREIGN KEY (store_id)
    REFERENCES shopping.shopping_stores(id) ON DELETE SET NULL

-- RESTRICT (default): cannot delete a user who created lists
CONSTRAINT fk_shopping_lists_user FOREIGN KEY (created_by)
    REFERENCES profiles.user(id)

docs.title: 8. Seed Data
docs.text:
Reference data (categories, stores, default roles) is managed in dedicated seed changesets using idempotent inserts:

INSERT INTO shopping.shopping_categories (name, description, icon) VALUES
('Fruits & Vegetables', 'Fresh produce', 'IconApple'),
('Dairy & Eggs', 'Milk, cheese, yogurt', 'IconEgg')
ON CONFLICT (name) DO NOTHING;

Seed Data Rules

  • Separate seed data from DDL in its own changeset file.
  • Use ON CONFLICT ... DO NOTHING for idempotency.
  • Only seed reference/lookup data — never seed transactional data.

docs.title: 9. JPA Alignment
docs.text:
Hibernate is configured with ddl-auto: validate — it verifies the schema on startup but never modifies it.

Schema Changes Must Go Through Liquibase

All DDL changes (tables, columns, indexes, constraints) MUST be added as Liquibase changesets. Never rely on Hibernate to create or alter schema objects. Entity annotations must match the Liquibase-managed schema exactly:

@Entity
@Table(name = "shopping_lists", schema = "shopping")  // Must match Liquibase DDL
public class ShoppingList {

docs.title: AI Alignment
docs.text:
Directives for AI Agents

  • ALL schema changes MUST be Liquibase changesets — never modify the database directly.
  • NEVER modify an applied changeset — always create new changesets.
  • ALWAYS include created_at, updated_at, and version on new tables.
  • ALWAYS use snake_case for all database identifiers.
  • ALWAYS add indexes on foreign key columns.
  • ALWAYS use BIGSERIAL for primary keys and BIGINT for foreign keys.
  • ALWAYS use DECIMAL(10,2) for monetary values — never FLOAT.
  • ALWAYS use ON CONFLICT ... DO NOTHING for seed data inserts.
  • ALWAYS match VARCHAR(n) lengths with backend Jakarta validation constraints.
  • PREFER ON DELETE CASCADE for parent-child, ON DELETE SET NULL for optional references.
  • ENSURE @Table(name, schema) annotations match the Liquibase-managed schema exactly.
  • ENSURE migrations are backward-compatible — no NOT NULL without defaults on existing columns.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This document defines the standards and patterns for writing frontend end-to-end (E2E) tests in the Home Application. E2E tests use Playwright with the Page Object Model.

docs.title: Test Structure
docs.text:
E2E tests use test.describe for feature grouping, test.beforeEach for API route mocking, and test.step with Given/When/Then labels for BDD readability.

test.describe('Shopping List Lifecycle Flow', () => {
  test.beforeEach(async ({ page }) => {
    await page.route('**/api/user/me', async (route) => {
      await route.fulfill({
        status: 200,
        contentType: 'application/json',
        body: JSON.stringify({ id: 1, email: 'user@test.com', ageGroupName: 'Adult' }),
      })
    })
  })

  test('Full lifecycle: Create -> Add Item -> Complete', async ({ page }) => {
    await test.step('Given the user is on the Shopping Lists page', async () => {
      await listsPage.goto()
    })

    await test.step('When the user creates a new list', async () => {
      await listsPage.createList('Weekly Groceries')
    })

    await test.step('Then the list should appear on the page', async () => {
      await expect(page.getByText('Weekly Groceries')).toBeVisible()
    })
  })
})

docs.title: Page Object Model
docs.text:
All E2E tests use Page Objects in e2e/pages/. Each class encapsulates locators and actions for a single page.

import { Page, Locator } from '@playwright/test'

export class ShoppingListsPage {
  readonly page: Page
  readonly title: Locator
  readonly newListButton: Locator

  constructor(page: Page) {
    this.page = page
    this.title = page.getByRole('heading', { name: /Shopping Lists/i })
    this.newListButton = page.getByRole('button', { name: /New List/i })
  }

  async goto() {
    await this.page.goto('/shopping/lists')
  }

  async createList(name: string) {
    await this.newListButton.click()
    // ...
  }
}

Page Object Conventions

  • One class per page: <Feature>Page.ts in e2e/pages/
  • Expose key elements as readonly Locator properties
  • Provide action methods (goto(), createList(), viewList())
  • Keep assertions in the spec file, not in the Page Object

docs.title: Test Behavior, Not Implementation
docs.text:
• Assert on visible text, form values, and element presence
  • Query by role, label, and text — the way a user would find elements
  • Never assert on CSS classes, component state, or internal method calls

docs.title: AI Alignment
docs.text:
Directives for AI Agents

  • USE Page Object Model for all E2E tests, placed in e2e/pages/.
  • USE test.step with Given/When/Then labels in E2E tests.
  • USE page.route() for API mocking in Playwright tests.
  • NEVER assert on CSS classes, internal state, or component internals.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This document defines the technical standards and architectural patterns for the React 19 frontend. The application uses Mantine 7 for UI, TanStack Query for server state, and TypeScript for type safety.

docs.title: 1. Project Structure
docs.text:
src/
├── App.tsx              # Providers, routing, layout
├── main.tsx             # Entry point, MantineProvider
├── theme.ts             # Mantine theme customization
├── components/          # Reusable UI components
│   ├── Layout.tsx       # Main shell with sidebar/header
│   ├── ProtectedRoute.tsx
│   ├── MarkdownContent.tsx
│   └── shopping/        # Domain-specific components
├── pages/               # Route-level components
│   ├── auth/
│   ├── dashboard/
│   ├── settings/
│   ├── shopping/
│   └── user/
├── services/
│   └── api.ts           # Centralized API layer
├── types/               # TypeScript interfaces
│   ├── api.ts
│   ├── shopping.ts
│   ├── settings.ts
│   ├── user.ts
│   └── index.ts         # Barrel re-export
├── utils/               # Pure utility functions
└── context/             # React Context providers
Folder Purpose pages/<domain>/ Route-level components that own data fetching and state components/<domain>/ Reusable, presentational UI components services/ API client and HTTP logic types/<domain>.ts TypeScript interfaces grouped by domain utils/ Pure helper functions with no side effects context/ React Context providers (e.g., AuthContext)

docs.title: Pages vs Components
docs.text:
Separation of Concerns

  • Pages (pages/) own data fetching (useQuery), mutations (useMutation), and state management.
  • Components (components/) are presentational — they receive data and callbacks via props.
  • Components in components/ must never call useQuery, useMutation, or fetch directly.

docs.title: Decomposing Pages
docs.text:
When a page grows complex, extract modals, list rows, and repeated UI into components/<domain>/. Each extracted component gets its own file and props interface.

pages/shopping/ShoppingListDetailsPage.tsx   ← owns data, state, mutations
components/shopping/ListItemRow.tsx           ← renders a single item row
components/shopping/EditListModal.tsx         ← edit form modal
components/shopping/AddItemModal.tsx          ← add item modal
components/shopping/PriceHistoryModal.tsx     ← price chart modal

When to Extract

Extract a component when it has a clear responsibility (a modal, a list row, a display widget) or when the page file exceeds ~300 lines.

docs.title: Props Interfaces
docs.text:
Every component defines a named <Component>Props interface directly above the component function.

interface ListItemRowProps {
  item: ShoppingListItem
  listStatus: string
  onToggleBought: (id: number, bought: boolean) => void
  onEdit: (item: ShoppingListItem) => void
  onRemove: (id: number) => void
}

export function ListItemRow({ item, listStatus, onToggleBought, onEdit, onRemove }: ListItemRowProps) {

docs.title: Named Exports
docs.text:
Export Convention

  • Use export function ComponentName — named exports improve refactoring, auto-imports, and searchability.
  • DO NOT use export default — the only exception is App.tsx.

docs.title: 3. TypeScript Conventions
docs.text:
Types are organized by domain in types/, with a barrel index.ts that re-exports everything.

File Content types/api.ts Shared types: ProblemDetail, ApiError, PagedResponse<T> types/shopping.ts ShoppingList, ShoppingItem, Coupon, LoyaltyCard, etc. types/user.ts UserProfile types/settings.ts AgeGroupConfig, FamilyRole, UserPreference types/index.ts Barrel: export * from './api', etc.

Type Rules

  • Use interface for object shapes — no I prefix (ShoppingList, not IShoppingList).
  • HATEOAS _links are typed as optional (_links?: { self: { href: string } }).
  • NEVER use any — use unknown and narrow with type guards if needed.

docs.title: 4. API Service Layer
docs.text:
All HTTP logic lives in services/api.ts. A private apiFetch wrapper handles cross-cutting concerns:

  • CSRF — extracts XSRF-TOKEN from cookies and attaches it as X-XSRF-TOKEN header on mutating requests
  • Error Handling — parses RFC 7807 ProblemDetail responses and shows Mantine toast notifications on failure
  • Auth — 401 responses are silently passed through for the auth layer to handle
// Public API functions — one per operation
export async function fetchLists(): Promise<ShoppingList[]> { ... }
export async function createList(data: Partial<ShoppingList>): Promise<ShoppingList> { ... }
export async function deleteList(id: number): Promise<void> { ... }

Single Source of Truth

All API calls MUST go through apiFetch. Never call fetch() directly from components or pages — this bypasses CSRF, error handling, and notification logic.

docs.title: Queries
docs.text:
Use useQuery with domain-specific query keys. The key should reflect the resource being fetched.

const { data: lists, isLoading } = useQuery({
  queryKey: ['shopping-lists'],
  queryFn: fetchLists,
})

docs.title: Mutations
docs.text:
Use useMutation with onSuccess to invalidate related queries and show a success notification.

const createMutation = useMutation({
  mutationFn: (values: typeof form.values) => createList(values),
  onSuccess: () => {
    queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['shopping-lists'] })
    notifications.show({ message: 'Shopping list created', color: 'green' })
    close()
  },
})

docs.title: Auth Context
docs.text:
AuthContext provides the current user via useQuery with a 5-minute staleTime. Components access it through the useAuth() hook.

const { user, isLoading, isError, logout } = useAuth()

Query Key Naming

Use descriptive, hierarchical keys: ['shopping-lists'], ['shopping-list', id], ['stores'], ['store', id, 'coupons'].

docs.title: Forms
docs.text:
Use @mantine/form with useForm and inline validation for all forms.

const form = useForm({
  initialValues: { name: '', description: '' },
  validate: {
    name: (value) => (value.length < 2 ? 'Name must have at least 2 characters' : null),
  },
})

docs.title: Modals
docs.text:
Use useDisclosure from @mantine/hooks for modal open/close state.

const [opened, { open, close }] = useDisclosure(false)

docs.title: Notifications
docs.text:
Use notifications.show() from @mantine/notifications for user feedback after mutations.

notifications.show({ message: 'List deleted', color: 'green' })

docs.title: Icons
docs.text:
Use @tabler/icons-react exclusively. Do not mix icon libraries.

import { IconPlus, IconTrash, IconEdit } from '@tabler/icons-react'

docs.title: Styling
docs.text:
Prefer Mantine Style Props

Use Mantine's built-in style props (p, m, bg, c, fw, gap, etc.) over inline style objects. They are theme-aware and responsive-friendly. Only fall back to style for truly custom CSS that Mantine doesn't cover.

// ✅ Prefer
<Stack gap="md" p="lg" bg="gray.0">

// ❌ Avoid
<Stack style={{ gap: 16, padding: 24, backgroundColor: '#f8f9fa' }}>

docs.title: Provider Hierarchy
docs.text:
main.tsx:  MantineProvider → ModalsProvider → App
App.tsx:   QueryClientProvider → AuthProvider → ModalsProvider → Notifications → BrowserRouter

docs.title: Protected Routes
docs.text:
The ProtectedRoute component checks auth state and redirects unauthenticated users to /login.

<Route
  path="/"
  element={
    <ProtectedRoute>
      <Layout />
    </ProtectedRoute>
  }
>
  <Route index element={<Dashboard />} />
  <Route path="shopping">
    <Route path="lists" element={<ShoppingListsPage />} />
    <Route path="lists/:id" element={<ShoppingListDetailsPage />} />
  </Route>
</Route>

Route Grouping

Routes are nested by domain (shopping, settings, user). New features should follow this pattern rather than adding flat top-level routes.

docs.title: 8. Utilities
docs.text:
Pure helper functions live in utils/. They must have no side effects and include JSDoc.

/**
 * Determine the correct image source for photos.
 * Handles direct URLs, data URIs, and base64 strings.
 */
export const getPhotoSrc = (photo: string | undefined | null): string | null => {
  if (!photo) return null
  if (photo.startsWith('http') || photo.startsWith('data:image')) return photo
  if (photo.startsWith('/')) return photo
  return `data:image/png;base64,${photo}`
}

Utility Rules

  • Pure functions only — no side effects, no imports from React or Mantine.
  • Include JSDoc describing the function's purpose.

docs.title: 9. Code Quality
docs.text:
Tool Configuration Purpose TypeScript strict mode Type safety ESLint recommended + strict + stylistic Code quality Prettier No semicolons, single quotes, trailing commas, 100 char width Formatting react-hooks ESLint plugin Hook rules enforcement react-refresh ESLint plugin HMR compatibility

Automated Formatting

Formatting is enforced by Prettier via ESLint. Do not override Prettier rules in individual files. Run npm run lint to check compliance.

docs.title: AI Alignment
docs.text:
Directives for AI Agents

  • ALWAYS use named exports for components and pages — no export default.
  • ALWAYS define a <Component>Props interface for component props.
  • ALWAYS place pages in pages/<domain>/ and reusable components in components/<domain>/.
  • ALWAYS use TanStack Query for data fetching — never fetch in useEffect.
  • ALWAYS use apiFetch from services/api.ts — never call fetch directly.
  • ALWAYS invalidate relevant queries after mutations.
  • ALWAYS use notifications.show() for user feedback after mutations.
  • ALWAYS use @tabler/icons-react for icons — do not mix icon libraries.
  • ALWAYS define types in types/<domain>.ts — never use any.
  • PREFER Mantine style props over inline style objects.
  • KEEP components in components/ pure — no useQuery, no direct API calls.
  • EXTRACT modals, list rows, and repeated UI from pages into components/<domain>/.
  • USE useForm for all form state and validation.
  • USE useDisclosure for modal open/close state.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This document defines the technical standards and best practices for the Spring Boot 4.0 backend. We leverage the latest features of Java 25 to produce code that is expressive, type-safe, and highly performant.

docs.title: Records and DTOs
docs.text:
Use Records for simple, read-only value objects that carry no validation or framework annotations. For DTOs that require Jakarta validation, HATEOAS @Relation, or mutability, use Lombok @Data classes.

Best Practices

  • Use Records for embedded value objects (e.g., Category, Store, Barcode).
  • Use @Data classes for DTOs that need @NotBlank, @Size, @Pattern, or @Relation.
  • Leverage record patterns in instanceof and switch.
  • Group related attributes into nested objects. Avoid flat attributes like storeId and storeName. Instead, use a nested Store object with id and name fields.
  • DO NOT use records for JPA Entities (Entities require identity and proxying).
  • DO NOT use records for DTOs that require Jakarta validation annotations.

DTO Grouping

Avoid flat structure with duplicated entity data:

public class ShoppingListDTO {
    private Long id;
    private String name;
    private Long storeId;      // ❌ Flat attribute
    private String storeName;  // ❌ Flat attribute
}

Prefer nested objects with grouped entity data:

public class ShoppingListDTO {
    private Long id;
    private String name;
    private Store store;       // ✅ Nested object
}

public record Store(Long id, String name) {}
Record (Value Object)DTO (@Data Class) 
public record Store(
    Long id,
    String name
) {}

@Data
@Relation(collectionRelation = "userProfiles", itemRelation = "userProfile")
public class UserProfileDTO {

    private Long id;

    @NotBlank(message = "Email is required")
    @Email(message = "Email must be valid")
    private String email;

    @Size(max = 50, message = "First name must not exceed 50 characters")
    private String firstName;
}

docs.title: Pattern Matching
docs.text:
Leverage pattern matching to reduce boilerplate and improve readability when dealing with complex types.

Switch Expressions

Use exhaustive switch expressions with pattern matching for business logic branching.

String statusMessage = switch (user.getAgeGroup()) {
    case CHILD -> "Supervised access only";
    case TEENAGER -> "Standard access";
    case ADULT -> "Full administrative access";
};

docs.title: Sealed Classes
docs.text:
Use sealed interfaces and classes to define restricted class hierarchies, especially for domain-specific states or result types.

public sealed interface CalculationResult 
    permits Success, Failure {}

docs.title: Virtual Threads (Project Loom)
docs.text:
Spring Boot 4.0 is optimized for Virtual Threads. We prefer simple blocking I/O on virtual threads over complex reactive programming for most use cases.

Scaling Strategy

  • Prefer spring.threads.virtual.enabled=true.
  • Avoid ThreadLocal in favor of Scoped Values when using structured concurrency.

docs.title: Functional Style
docs.text:
Maintain a strong preference for functional programming patterns to handle collections and optionality.

  • Streams: Use for all collection transformations. Avoid manual loops.
  • Optionals: Use for return types that may be absent. Avoid returning null.
  • Method References: Prefer User::getName over u -> u.getName().

docs.title: Controller Patterns (HATEOAS)
docs.text:
All controllers MUST return HATEOAS-compliant resources using HAL.

  • Use RepresentationModelAssembler to decouple entity-to-resource mapping.
  • Always include a self link for every returned resource.
  • Use @RequiredArgsConstructor for dependency injection.
  • Use @AuthenticationPrincipal OAuth2User principal to access the current user.
HTTP Method Response Pattern GET Return Resource or CollectionModel<Resource> directly POST @ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.CREATED) — return Resource PUT/PATCH Return updated Resource directly DELETE ResponseEntity.noContent().build()

Resource Package Structure

Resource classes and assemblers live in controller/<domain>/resource/<subdomain>/:

  • <Domain>Resource — extends RepresentationModel
  • <Domain>ResourceAssembler — extends RepresentationModelAssemblerSupport

docs.title: JPA Entity Design
docs.text:
• Audit Columns: Every entity must use @CreationTimestamp for createdAt and @UpdateTimestamp for updatedAt.
  • Fetch Strategy: Default to FetchType.LAZY for all relationships.
  • Lombok: Use @Data + @EqualsAndHashCode(onlyExplicitlyIncluded = true) on all entities.

Circular Reference Prevention

@EqualsAndHashCode(onlyExplicitlyIncluded = true) is mandatory on every entity. Without it, bidirectional JPA relationships cause infinite loops in hashCode() and toString(). Annotate the identity field with @EqualsAndHashCode.Include.

@Entity
@Table(name = "user", schema = "profiles")
@Data
@EqualsAndHashCode(onlyExplicitlyIncluded = true)
public class User {

    @Id
    @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.IDENTITY)
    @EqualsAndHashCode.Include
    private Long id;
}

docs.title: Adapter Pattern (Entity ↔ DTO)
docs.text:
Static adapter classes handle all conversions between JPA entities and DTOs. They live in model/adapter/<schema>/ and follow the naming convention <Domain>Adapter.

Adapter Rules

  • Use static methods (toDTO, toEntity) — adapters are stateless.
  • Place in model/adapter/<schema>/ matching the entity's schema.
  • Entities must never be exposed directly in API responses — always convert via an adapter.
public class UserProfileAdapter {

    public static UserProfileDTO toDTO(User user) {
        if (user == null) return null;
        UserProfileDTO dto = new UserProfileDTO();
        dto.setId(user.getId());
        dto.setEmail(user.getEmail());
        // ...
        return dto;
    }
}

docs.title: Service Layer Patterns
docs.text:
Services are the transactional boundary and the home for all business logic.

Best Practices

  • Annotate the class with @Service, @RequiredArgsConstructor, @Transactional, and @Validated.
  • Mark read-only methods with @Transactional(readOnly = true).
  • Use @Valid on method parameters to trigger Jakarta validation.
  • Throw domain exceptions (ObjectNotFoundException, ValidationException) — never return null for missing entities.
  • DO NOT use @Autowired — rely on constructor injection via @RequiredArgsConstructor.
@Service
@RequiredArgsConstructor
@Transactional
@Validated
public class UserProfileService {

    private final UserRepository userRepository;

    @Transactional(readOnly = true)
    public Optional<UserProfileDTO> getUserProfile(Long id) {
        return userRepository.findById(id).map(UserProfileAdapter::toDTO);
    }
}

docs.title: 4. Error Handling (RFC 7807)
docs.text:
All exceptions are mapped to RFC 7807 Problem Detail responses by a centralized GlobalExceptionHandler annotated with @RestControllerAdvice. Error type URIs are built from the AppErrorType enum and the configurable app.error-base-url property.

Service Layer Rule

Services throw domain-specific exceptions. The GlobalExceptionHandler maps them to the correct HTTP status and Problem Detail response — services should never construct HTTP responses directly.

docs.title: Exception Mapping
docs.text:
Exception HTTP Status Description MethodArgumentNotValidException 400 @Valid controller parameter failures ConstraintViolationException 400 @Validated service parameter failures ValidationException 400 Business validation errors HttpMessageNotReadableException 400 Malformed or unreadable request body ObjectNotFoundException 404 Entity not found DataAccessResourceFailureException 503 Database unreachable HomeAppException 500 Generic application error Exception (catch-all) 500 Unexpected errors

Spring Security Exceptions

AccessDeniedException must be re-thrown in the catch-all handler so it propagates to the Spring Security filter chain. Swallowing it would bypass authorization enforcement.

docs.title: Exception Design
docs.text:
Best Practices

  • Never throw or catch generic Exception or RuntimeException. Create specialized exceptions that extend HomeAppException.
  • Catch specific exceptions. Never use catch (Exception e) or catch (RuntimeException e).
  • Create domain-specific exceptions for distinct error conditions (e.g., ObjectNotFoundException, ValidationException, PhotoDownloadException).
  • All custom exceptions MUST extend HomeAppException and include an AppErrorType.

Custom Exception

@ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND)
public class ObjectNotFoundException extends HomeAppException {

    public ObjectNotFoundException(String entity, Long id) {
        super("%s with id %d not found".formatted(entity, id));
    }
}

Response Status Annotation

Every custom exception exposed to REST controllers MUST have a @ResponseStatus annotation to set the appropriate HTTP status code. The GlobalExceptionHandler will then map it correctly without additional configuration.

Anti-Pattern

NEVER do this:

try {
    // ...
} catch (Exception e) {           // ❌ Generic catch
    throw new RuntimeException(e); // ❌ Wrapped in generic exception
}

docs.title: 5. Coding Style & Clean Code
docs.text:
• Local Variable Type Inference: Use var for local variables where the type is obvious from the right-hand side.
  • String Formatting: Use String.format() or "text %s".formatted(value) for complex string construction. Use simple concatenation for trivial cases.
  • Exhaustive Logic: Ensure all switch expressions and logic chains cover all possible states.

docs.title: Javadoc
docs.text:
Javadoc Standards

  • Class-level Javadoc is mandatory on all public classes (services, controllers, entities, adapters).
  • Use @param and @return tags on all public methods.
  • Use {@link ClassName} to reference related classes and {@code value} for inline code.

docs.title: Admonition Formatting
docs.text:
Best Practices

  • Always add an empty line between the admonition title and the body content. Without this line, Material for MkDocs treats the first line as plain text and fails to render the admonition correctly.

Correct vs Incorrect

Incorrect (no empty line — renders as plain text):

!!! success "Best Practices"
- :material-check-all: Extend `JpaRepository<Entity, Long>`.

Correct (empty line after title, proper indentation — renders properly):

!!! success "Best Practices"

    - :material-check-all: Extend `JpaRepository<Entity, Long>`.

docs.title: Class & Method Design
docs.text:
Best Practices

  • No god classes. Avoid overly large or complex classes. Break responsibilities into focused, well-named classes with proper documentation.
  • Keep methods short. Each method should do one thing. Extract helper methods to improve readability, testability, and maintainability.
  • Document intent. Non-trivial logic must have clear Javadoc or inline comments explaining why, not just what.
  • No FQDN class references. Always import classes instead of using fully qualified names (e.g., java.util.List). The only exception is when two imports would conflict (e.g., java.util.Date vs java.sql.Date).

docs.title: 6. Logging
docs.text:
Use Lombok's @Slf4j annotation for logging across all service classes. Always use parameterized messages — never string concatenation.

Best Practices

  • Add @Slf4j to every @Service class.
  • Use parameterized logging: log.warn("Failed for user {}", userId).
  • NEVER use string concatenation: log.warn("Failed for " + email).
Level Use Case Example debug Diagnostic detail, method entry/exit log.debug("Resolving profile for id {}", id) info Significant business events log.info("New user created: {}", userId) warn Recoverable failures log.warn("Photo download failed for user {}: {}", userId, e.getMessage()) error Unexpected exceptions log.error("Unexpected failure in scheduled task", e)

Sensitive Data

Never log PII (emails, phone numbers), tokens, or credentials. Use surrogate identifiers (e.g., user ID) instead.

docs.title: 7. Repository Patterns
docs.text:
Repositories are Spring Data JPA interfaces that provide data access for a single entity.

Best Practices

  • Extend JpaRepository<Entity, Long>.
  • Prefer derived query methods (findByEmail) over @Query when the method name is readable.
  • Return Page<T> for any operation that lists multiple records.
  • Place in repository.<schema>/ matching the entity's database schema.
  • Add Javadoc on custom query methods explaining the business intent.
public interface UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Long> {

    /** Finds a user by their unique email address. */
    Optional<User> findByEmail(String email);
}

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
All /api/** endpoints require a valid OAuth2 session. Non-authenticated requests return 401 Unauthorized.

Default Access Rule

Authentication is enforced globally by SecurityConfig. Every new endpoint under /api/** is protected automatically — no per-controller annotation is needed for basic auth.

docs.title: Authorization (Method Security)
docs.text:
Use @PreAuthorize for endpoints that require role-based restrictions beyond basic authentication.

When to Use @PreAuthorize

Only add @PreAuthorize when an endpoint must be restricted to a specific role. If all authenticated users should have access, the default security config is sufficient.

  • Role Naming Convention: ROLE_<AGE_GROUP> (e.g., ROLE_ADULT, ROLE_TEENAGER).
  • Current User: Access via @AuthenticationPrincipal OAuth2User principal.
@RestController
@RequestMapping("/api/settings")
@RequiredArgsConstructor
@PreAuthorize("hasRole('ROLE_ADULT')")
public class SettingsController {
    // Only adults can access settings management
}

docs.title: AI Alignment
docs.text:
Directives for AI Agents

  • Use Records for simple value objects; use @Data classes for DTOs requiring validation or @Relation.
  • ALWAYS use @Data + @EqualsAndHashCode(onlyExplicitlyIncluded = true) on JPA entities.
  • NEVER expose entities in API responses — always convert via a static Adapter class.
  • ALWAYS annotate services with @Service, @RequiredArgsConstructor, @Transactional, and @Validated.
  • Use @Transactional(readOnly = true) on read-only service methods.
  • Throw domain exceptions (ObjectNotFoundException, ValidationException) — never return null for missing entities.
  • NEVER use @Autowired — rely on constructor injection via @RequiredArgsConstructor.
  • PRIORITIZE Streams for collection manipulation and Optional over null checks.
  • Add @Slf4j to all service classes. Use parameterized logging. Never log sensitive data.
  • Repositories extend JpaRepository, use derived query methods, and return Page<T> for lists.
  • Use @PreAuthorize with ROLE_<AGE_GROUP> for role-restricted endpoints.
  • ENSURE all new entities follow the Liquibase naming conventions established in the Database Design.
  • NEVER create god classes — break large classes into focused, single-responsibility components.
  • NEVER use fully qualified class names in code — always use imports. Only use FQDN when resolving an import conflict.
  • GROUP related entity attributes into nested objects in DTOs. Never use flat attributes like storeId + storeName — use Store store with nested id and name.
  • NEVER throw or catch generic Exception or RuntimeException. Create specialized exceptions extending HomeAppException for each distinct error condition.
  • ALWAYS annotate custom exceptions with @ResponseStatus to set the HTTP status code (e.g., @ResponseStatus(HttpStatus.NOT_FOUND)).
  • ALWAYS add an empty line between admonition titles and their content (e.g., !!! success "Title"\n\n- item).

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: This document defines the standards and patterns for writing Spock Framework tests in the Home Application backend. All tests are written in Groovy and follow BDD (Behavior-Driven Development) conventions to produce expressive, readable specifications.

docs.title: Title and Narrative
docs.text:
Every spec class MUST include @Title and @Narrative annotations.

Best Practices

  • @Title — short, descriptive name of the component or feature.
  • @Narrative — user story format: As a... I want... So that...
@Title("User Profile Service")
@Narrative("""
As a user
I want to retrieve and update my profile information
So that my account details are always accurate and up to date
""")
class UserProfileServiceSpec extends BaseIntegrationTest {

docs.title: @Subject Annotation
docs.text:
Mark the primary bean under test with @Subject to distinguish it from supporting dependencies.

@Autowired
@Subject
ShoppingCatalogService shoppingCatalogService

@Autowired
ShoppingCategoryRepository categoryRepository  // supporting dependency

docs.title: Block Descriptions
docs.text:
given, when, then, and and blocks MUST always contain a descriptive label. Code inside blocks must be indented.

Readable Specs

Avoid long monolithic blocks. Split then into logical and blocks with clear descriptions.

def "GET /api/user should return paginated list of profiles"() {
    when: "requesting all user profiles"
        def response = mockMvc.perform(get("/api/user"))

    then: "response status and content type are correct"
        response.andExpect(status().isOk())
                .andExpect(content().contentType(HAL_JSON_VALUE))

    and: "the list contains the expected users"
        response.andExpect(jsonPath('$._embedded.userProfiles').isArray())
                .andExpect(jsonPath('$._embedded.userProfiles.length()').value(2))

    and: "pagination metadata is correct"
        response.andExpect(jsonPath('$.page.totalElements').value(2))
}

docs.title: Method Naming
docs.text:
Test method names should read as natural language specifications.

Pattern Example Positive case "should return user profile when user exists" Negative case "should throw ObjectNotFoundException when user does not exist" API endpoint "GET /api/user should return paginated list of profiles" Conditional "should default to Adult if birthdate is null" Security "should deny access to Child for retrieving age groups"

docs.title: Base Integration Test
docs.text:
All integration tests extend BaseIntegrationTest, which provides a shared Testcontainers PostgreSQL 17 instance.

@Testcontainers
abstract class BaseIntegrationTest extends Specification {

    @Container
    @ServiceConnection
    @Shared
    static PostgreSQLContainer<?> postgres = new PostgreSQLContainer<>("postgres:17")
            .withDatabaseName("mydatabase")
            .withUsername("myuser")
            .withPassword("secret")
}

Never Override the Container

The @Shared static container is reused across all specs in the test suite. Do not create additional PostgreSQL containers in individual specs.

docs.title: Standard Annotation Stacks
3. Transaction & Data Management
docs.text:
Best Practices

  • Use @Transactional on the spec class — each test runs in a transaction that is rolled back automatically.
  • Use @Sql to load test data from SQL scripts.
  • AVOID cleanup blocks for data teardown — @Transactional rollback handles this.

docs.title: SQL Test Data
docs.text:
Use @Sql on individual test methods to seed data. Scripts live in src/test/resources/scripts/sql/.

@Sql("/scripts/sql/user-profile-test-data.sql")
def "GET /api/user should return paginated list of profiles"() {

Script Naming Convention

Name scripts after the feature they support: <feature>-test-data.sql (e.g., user-profile-test-data.sql, pagination-test-data.sql).

docs.title: 4. Mocking Strategy
docs.text:
Avoid mocking as much as possible. Use @Autowired to inject real Spring-managed beans backed by Testcontainers.

When to Mock

Only mock when the real component is impractical to use in tests:

  • External HTTP APIs — use WireMock to simulate third-party services.
  • Side effects — use @MockitoBean for services with irreversible side effects.
  • AVOID mocking repositories, services, or adapters that can run against Testcontainers.

docs.title: WireMock for External APIs
docs.text:
wireMockServer.stubFor(get(urlPathEqualTo("/people/me"))
        .withHeader("Authorization", equalTo("Bearer " + token))
        .willReturn(aResponse()
                .withHeader("Content-Type", "application/json")
                .withBody("""{ "birthdays": [{ "date": { "year": 1990, "month": 5, "day": 15 } }] }""")))

docs.title: @MockitoBean for Side Effects
docs.text:
@MockitoBean
PhotoService photoService

// In test:
when(photoService.downloadAndConvertToBase64(pictureUrl)).thenReturn("base64-mock-data")

docs.title: 5. Data-Driven Testing
docs.text:
Use where blocks with table notation for parameterized tests. Combine with @Unroll to generate individual test reports per data row.

Best Practices

  • Use @Unroll with #variable placeholders in the method name.
  • Use table notation in where blocks for readability.
  • Add a descriptive label to the where: block.
@Unroll
def "should classify user correctly based on age: #ageYears years old"() {
    given: "a birthdate calculated from age"
        def birthdate = LocalDate.now().minusYears(ageYears)

    when: "classifying the birthdate"
        def result = ageClassificationService.classify(birthdate)

    then: "the correct age group is returned"
        result == expectedGroup

    where: "various ages and their expected groups"
        ageYears | expectedGroup
        5        | "Child"
        13       | "Teenager"
        18       | "Adult"
}

docs.title: Validation Testing
docs.text:
def "PUT /api/user/{id} should return 400 when #field is #scenario"() {
    given: "an update request with #scenario"
        def requestBody = validUpdateRequestBody() + [(field): value]

    when: "updating user profile with invalid data"
        def response = mockMvc.perform(put("/api/user/1")
                .contentType(MediaType.APPLICATION_JSON)
                .content(JsonOutput.toJson(requestBody)))

    then: "400 ProblemDetail is returned with specific field error"
        response.andExpect(status().isBadRequest())
                .andExpect(jsonPath("\$.errors.${field}").value(message))

    where:
        field       | scenario          | value          | message
        "email"     | "missing"         | null           | "Email is required"
        "email"     | "invalid format"  | "not-an-email" | "Email must be valid"
        "firstName" | "too long"        | "a" * 51       | "First name must not exceed 50 characters"
}

docs.title: OAuth2 Authentication
docs.text:
Use oauth2Login() to simulate authenticated users with specific attributes.

given: "an authenticated user session"
    def auth = oauth2Login().attributes { it.put("email", "user@example.com") }

when: "requesting the 'me' endpoint"
    def response = mockMvc.perform(get("/api/user/me").with(auth))

docs.title: Role-Based Access Control
docs.text:
Use user().roles() to test @PreAuthorize restrictions. Always test both positive and negative cases.

def "should allow Adult to retrieve age groups"() {
    when: "an Adult user requests age groups"
        def response = mockMvc.perform(get("/api/settings/age-groups")
                .with(user("adult").roles("ADULT")))

    then: "access is granted"
        response.andExpect(status().isOk())
}

def "should deny access to Child for retrieving age groups"() {
    when: "a Child user requests age groups"
        def response = mockMvc.perform(get("/api/settings/age-groups")
                .with(user("child").roles("CHILD")))

    then: "access is forbidden"
        response.andExpect(status().isForbidden())
}

docs.title: with Block (Fail-Fast)
docs.text:
Use with for asserting multiple properties on a single object. Stops at the first failure.

then: "user profile is returned with correct data"
    result.isPresent()
    with(result.get()) {
        email == "user@example.com"
        firstName == "John"
        photo == "photo.jpg"
    }

docs.title: verifyAll Block (Soft Assertions)
docs.text:
Use verifyAll when you want to see all failures at once rather than stopping at the first one. Ideal for adapter tests and data mapping verification.

then: "DTO is correctly populated"
    verifyAll(result) {
        email == "user@example.com"
        firstName == "John"
        photo == "photo.jpg"
    }

When to Use Each

  • with — default choice for most assertions. Fail-fast is useful during debugging.
  • verifyAll — prefer when checking many independent fields (e.g., adapter/mapper tests) where seeing all mismatches at once saves time.

docs.title: expect Block
docs.text:
Use expect for simple, single-expression tests that have no setup or stimulus.

def "toDTO should return null when user is null"() {
    expect: "null input returns null"
        UserProfileAdapter.toDTO(null) == null
}

docs.title: Exception Assertions
docs.text:
Use thrown() to verify expected exceptions.

when: "updating non-existent user"
    userProfileService.updateUserProfile(dto)

then: "ObjectNotFoundException is thrown"
    thrown(ObjectNotFoundException)

docs.title: MockMvc + JsonPath
docs.text:
Use jsonPath for API response assertions. Always verify status, content type, and body.

then: "200 OK with HAL JSON is returned"
    response.andExpect(status().isOk())
            .andExpect(content().contentType(HAL_JSON_VALUE))

and: "HATEOAS links are present"
    response.andExpect(jsonPath('$._links.self.href').value(containsString("/api/user/${id}")))

docs.title: 8. Helper Methods
docs.text:
Extract reusable test data builders as private methods at the bottom of the spec class.

private Map validUpdateRequestBody() {
    [
        email: "valid@example.com",
        firstName: "John",
        lastName: "Doe",
        enabled: true
    ]
}

Groovy Map Syntax

Use Groovy's map literal syntax for building request bodies — it's concise and converts cleanly to JSON via JsonOutput.toJson().

docs.title: AI Alignment
docs.text:
Directives for AI Agents

  • ALWAYS include @Title and @Narrative annotations on every spec class.
  • ALWAYS add descriptive labels to given, when, then, and and blocks.
  • ALWAYS extend BaseIntegrationTest for integration tests.
  • ALWAYS use @Subject on the primary bean under test.
  • ALWAYS use @Transactional for automatic rollback — never use cleanup blocks for data teardown.
  • ALWAYS use @Sql scripts for test data seeding, placed in src/test/resources/scripts/sql/.
  • PREFER real injected components over mocks. Only mock external APIs (WireMock) or side-effect services (@MockitoBean).
  • USE @Unroll with where blocks for data-driven tests.
  • USE with for fail-fast assertions, verifyAll for soft assertions on many fields.
  • USE expect for simple single-expression tests with no setup.
  • USE oauth2Login() for authenticated API tests and user().roles() for RBAC tests.
  • SPLIT long then blocks into logical and blocks with clear descriptions.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
High-quality documentation is a first-class citizen in the Home Application ecosystem. This document defines the general standards, visual patterns, and workflows for maintaining professional, interactive documentation.

Spec-Driven Development (SDD)

For requirements syntax (EARS), persistent anchors, and methodology-specific standards, refer to the SDD Guidelines.

docs.title: Directory Structure
docs.text:
All documentation source files are stored in the docs/ directory and written in GitHub Flavored Markdown.

Location Content Type docs/help/ User-facing guides and environment setup tutorials. docs/development/ Technical guidelines, coding standards, and AI context. docs/specification/requirements/ Functional and Non-Functional requirements. docs/specification/design/ Architectural design, ER diagrams, and UI patterns. docs/specification/roadmap/ Transactional task files and feature backlogs.

docs.title: Visual Patterns & UI
docs.text: We leverage MkDocs Material features to provide a modern, interactive experience.

docs.title: 1. Admonitions
docs.text:
Use admonitions to categorize information and improve scannability.

  • !!! success: Used for positive results or criteria (See SDD for Requirements usage).
  • !!! quote: Used for rationale or citations.
  • !!! info: General information, definitions, or User Journeys.
  • !!! abstract: Architectural decisions or high-level summaries.
  • !!! warning: Critical security or operational notices.
  • !!! tip: Useful hints, shortcuts, or implementation advice.

docs.title: 2. Layout Elements
docs.text:
• Grid Cards: Use <div class="grid cards" markdown> for high-level overviews or feature lists.
  • Tabs: Use content tabs (=== "Title") to separate Backend, Frontend, or specific technical layers.
  • Icons: Use integrated icons (e.g., :material-check-all:, :simple-spring:) to provide visual cues.

docs.title: 1. Diagrams
docs.text: Use Mermaid.js for all architectural and database diagrams. - Use erDiagram for individual schema details. - Use flowchart TB with subgraph for high-level multi-domain overviews.

docs.title: 2. File Naming
docs.text:
• Use kebab-case for file and directory names (e.g., user-profile.md).
  • Group related files into logical sub-directories.

docs.title: Build & Validation
docs.text: The documentation site is generated as a static HTML artifact.

docs.title: Build Command
docs.text: ./scripts/generate-docs.sh

docs.title: Handling Build Output
docs.text:
• Warnings: MkDocs warnings regarding broken links or missing anchors MUST be fixed immediately.
  • Navigation: Ensure any new page is explicitly added to the nav section of mkdocs.yml.
  • Dead Links: Use the build log to identify and repair incorrect relative paths.

docs.title: AI Alignment
docs.text: AI agents assisting in documentation updates MUST: 1. Run the build script to validate link integrity before completing a task. 2. Maintain visual consistency by using established admonition and layout patterns. 3. Prioritize clarity and brevity in help guides and tutorials.

docs.title: The SDD Philosophy
docs.text: Spec-Driven Development (SDD) is a documentation-first methodology where requirements, architectural design, and implementation plans are formalized in structured markdown before any code is written.

docs.title: 1. Requirements Documentation
docs.text: Requirements define What needs to be built. They are categorized into User Journeys (UJ), Functional Requirements (FR), and Non-Functional Requirements (NFR).

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
• EARS Syntax: Every requirement MUST use the structured EARS templates (see legend below).
  • Short Anchors: Every header MUST include a unique, short ID anchor: ### FR-1: Name {: #fr-1 }.
  • Rationale: The business value (So That...) MUST be placed in a !!! quote "Rationale" block after the criteria.

docs.title: EARS Trigger Legend
docs.text:
• Ubiquitous: "The system shall..." (Always true)
  • Event-driven: "When , the system shall..."
  • Unwanted Behavior: "If , then the system shall..."
  • State-driven: "While , the system shall..."
  • Optional: "Where , the system shall..."

docs.title: 2. Design Documentation
docs.text: Design defines How the requirements will be implemented. It covers architecture, database schemas, and UI patterns.

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
• Mermaid Diagrams: Use diagrams for flows, hierarchies, and ER relationship.
  • Traceability: Link design components back to specific FR/NFR anchors.
  • Problem Detail: Always document error states using the RFC 7807 format.

docs.title: 3. Roadmap Index
docs.text: The Roadmap (docs/specification/roadmap/index.md) defines the Sequence of Delivery. It provides a high-level view of milestones and the feature backlog.

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
• Milestone Table: Must track the sequential number, module link, and status icon.
  • Backlog Admonitions: Future features must be listed as !!! info blocks with a brief goal paragraph.

docs.title: Template: Roadmap Index
docs.text:
# Roadmap & Execution

## Active Roadmap
| Milestone | Module | Status |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **01** | [**Module Name**](01-slug.tasks.md) | :material-check-circle: Completed |

## Backlog
!!! info "Feature Name"
    Brief paragraph explaining goals.

docs.title: 4. Task Files (Execution Plans)
docs.text: Task files provide the Transactional Units of Work. Every milestone MUST have a corresponding [seq]-[slug].tasks.md file.

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
• User Stories: Follow the format: As a [Persona], I want [Action], so that [Value].
  • Requirement Note: Every story MUST have a !!! note "Validates Requirements" block.
  • Dependencies: Explicitly link to previous stories within the same or other task files.
  • DoD: Every task file MUST end with a "Definition of Done" quality checklist.

docs.title: Template: Task File
docs.text:
# Tasks: [Epic Name]

## 1. User Stories
### US-1: Story Title {: #us-1 }
!!! abstract "Story Definition"
    **As a** [User], **I want** [Goal], **so that** [Value].
    - **Persona:** [Engineer Type]
    - **Estimate:** [X] hours
    - **Dependencies:** [US-X](#us-x)

!!! note "Validates Requirements"
    - [:material-link: **FR-1: Name**](../requirements/file.md#fr-1)

!!! success "Acceptance Criteria"
    1. :material-check-all:{ title="Ubiquitous" } Criterion...

!!! example "Implementation Tasks"
    - [ ] :material-file-code: `Path/to/file.java`

docs.title: 5. Test Strategy & Scenarios
docs.text: Test documentation defines Validation. It ensures the implementation matches the spec.

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
• Test Scenarios (TS): Use Given/When/Then syntax with icons.
  • Icon Mapping: :material-check-circle: for Positive cases, :material-alert-circle: for Negative cases.
  • Requirement Linkage: Every TS MUST have a !!! info "Validates" block.

docs.title: 7. Modularization Strategy
docs.text: As the application grows, documentation for a single domain may become too large for a single file. In these cases, documents MUST be broken down into separate modules.

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
• Folder per Domain: Create a sub-directory for the domain (e.g., docs/specification/requirements/).
  • The Orchestrator: The root file (e.g., requirements.md or index.md) MUST behave as an index and high-level overview.
  • Granularity: Each sub-file should focus on a specific sub-domain (e.g., auth-profile.md, shopping-list.md).
  • Grid Navigation: Index files should use MkDocs Grid Cards to provide a visual and descriptive entry point to sub-modules.

docs.title: Template: Domain Index File
docs.text:
# [Domain] Requirements Index

## Overview
Brief 1-2 paragraph description of the domain's scope.

<div class="grid cards" markdown>

-   :material-account-circle: **[Identity & Profiles](auth-profile.md)**

    User registration, profile management, and family role definition.

-   :material-shopping: **[Shopping Module](shopping-list.md)**

    Collaborative lists, price tracking, and store management.

</div>

---

## Shared Patterns
Document patterns that apply to ALL sub-modules here (e.g., audit columns, common UI behaviors).

docs.title: 9. Execution Tracking & Persistence
docs.text: To ensure continuity across sessions and allow for seamless handovers between AI agents (or after context clears), the Task File acts as the persistent "source of truth" for current progress.

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
• Stateless Operation: AI agents MUST treat the local task file as their primary memory. Before starting work, the agent must read the file to identify the next pending task.
  • Atomic Updates: Upon completing a task or starting a new one, the agent MUST immediately update the task file to reflect the new state.
  • Granular States: Progress is tracked at two levels:
Level State Indicator User Story Completed :material-check-circle: in header In Progress :material-play-circle: in header Pending :material-clock-outline: in header Implementation Task Done - [x] In Progress - [/] (or - [ ] with agent mention) Pending - [ ]

docs.title: Workflow for AI Agents
docs.text:
1. Resume: Read the current tasks.md file. Find the first story marked as In Progress or the first unchecked Implementation Task.
  2. Act: Perform the required engineering work.
  3. Checkpoint: Update the task file by checking the box or changing the status icon.
  4. Handoff: If the session ends, the file state ensures the next agent knows exactly where to pick up.

docs.title: 8. Architectural Decision Records (ADR)
docs.text: ADRs document the "Why" behind major technical choices.

docs.title: Standards
docs.text:
• Short Anchors: Use {: #adr-x } for deep linking from design documents.
  • Rationale: Explicitly state alternatives considered.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: Welcome to the Home Application! This guide will walk you through setting up your development environment and running the application for the first time.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Ensuring you have the correct versions of these tools is critical for a smooth setup.

  • Java 25

    Required for the Spring Boot 4.0 backend.

  • Node.js 22+

    Required for the React frontend and Vite build system.

  • Docker & Compose

    Used for the PostgreSQL database and documentation build.

  • Google Cloud Console

    Required to generate OAuth2 credentials for authentication.

docs.title: 1. Clone & Enter
docs.text:
git clone https://github.com/jlmonteiro/home-application.git
cd home-application

docs.title: 2. Infrastructure
docs.text:
Start the PostgreSQL database container: 

docker compose up -d postgres

docs.title: 3. Environment Setup
docs.text:
Create a .env file in the root directory. You MUST provide valid Google OAuth2 credentials to enable login.

Security Notice

Never commit your .env file to version control. It is already included in .gitignore.

# Google OAuth2 credentials
export GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID="your-client-id.apps.googleusercontent.com"
export GOOGLE_CLIENT_SECRET="your-client-secret"

# Database (default settings)
export DATABASE_URL="jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/homeapp"
export DATABASE_USERNAME="homeapp"
export DATABASE_PASSWORD="homeapp"

# Frontend URL for CORS
export FRONTEND_URL="http://localhost:5173"

docs.title: Running the App
docs.text:
Backend Frontend
  1. Launch the API: 
    ./gradlew :home-app-backend:bootRun
  2. Verify: Access the HATEOAS root at http://localhost:8080/api
  1. Install & Start: 
    cd home-app-frontend
npm install
npm run dev
  2. Verify: Open http://localhost:5173 in your browser.

docs.title: Useful Commands
docs.text: Testing Coverage Documentation Module Command Purpose Backend ./gradlew :home-app-backend:test Run Spock integration tests. Frontend npm run test:e2e:mock Run Playwright E2E with MSW mocks. Module Command Purpose Backend ./gradlew :home-app-backend:jacocoTestReport Generate JaCoCo reports. Task Command Build Site ./scripts/generate-docs.sh Clean Build rm -rf build/site

docs.title: Project Structure
docs.text:
Organization

The project is a Gradle-managed monorepo, keeping frontend, backend, and documentation in a single atomic structure.

  • home-app-backend/: Spring Boot 4.0 API (Java 25).
  • :material-folder-layers: home-app-frontend/: React 19 SPA (Vite).
  • docs/: Markdown documentation source.
  • mkdocs.yml: Documentation site configuration.

docs.title: Troubleshooting
docs.text:
??? bug "Port 8080 or 5173 is already in use" Use lsof -i :8080 to find the process ID, then kill -9 <PID> to free the port.

??? bug "Database connection refused" Ensure the Docker container is running: docker compose ps. If it's down, try docker compose up -d.

??? bug "Google Login returns 'Redirect URI Mismatch'" Ensure your Google Cloud Console project has http://localhost:8080/login/oauth2/code/google added to the Authorized redirect URIs.

docs.title: User Profile
docs.text: Managing your personal information and family role is essential for a personalized experience within the Home Application.

docs.title: Editing Your Profile
docs.text:
Access the profile management page by following these simple steps:

  1. Avatar: Click your profile picture in the top-right corner of the application header.
  2. Action: Select "View/Edit Profile" from the dropdown menu.
  3. Update: Modify the desired fields and click the Save button.

docs.title: Profile Details
docs.text:
The profile is divided into mandatory identity fields and optional contact/social details.

  • Identity

    Includes your First Name, Last Name, and Email. These are synced from Google and are read-only.

  • Family Role

    Assign yourself a role (e.g., Mother, Father, Son) to help organize the household hierarchy.

  • Mobile Phone

    Optional contact number used for future notification features.

  • Social Links

    Connect your LinkedIn, Facebook, or Instagram profiles to share with your family members.

docs.title: Rationale & Privacy
docs.text:
Traceability

  • FR-4: Update User Profile: Defines the ability to customize identity and roles.
  • FR-3: User Profile Quick View: Powers the header dropdown summary.

Data Synchronization

The application periodically synchronizes your base identity (Name, Email, Photo) with your Google account to ensure your profile remains up-to-date without manual effort.

docs.title: Recipes & Meals
docs.text: The Recipes & Meals module is the family cookbook and meal planning hub. Create recipes, plan weekly meals, get reminders, and export ingredients directly to your shopping lists.

docs.title: Core Features
docs.text:
• Shared Cookbook

    Create recipes with photos, ingredients, preparation steps, and nutrition data. The whole family can comment and rate.

  • Weekly Meal Planner

    Plan meals for the week, assign recipes to family members, and notify the household for review.

  • Shopping Integration

    Export meal ingredients to a new or existing shopping list with a merge preview before applying.

  • Reminders & Notifications

    Get notified when a meal plan is published and receive preparation reminders before meal time.

docs.title: 1. Creating a Recipe
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Recipes & Meals Recipes.
  2. Click New Recipe and fill in the name, description (supports markdown), servings, and prep time.
  3. Optionally add a source link and video link for reference.
  4. Upload one or more photos and select a default photo for the recipes list.
  5. Add labels by typing — existing labels will autocomplete, new ones are created automatically.

docs.title: 2. Managing Ingredients
docs.text:
1. On the recipe detail page, click Add Ingredient.
  2. Search and select an item from the shopping catalog.
  3. Specify the quantity and unit (KG, G, L, ML, PACK, UNIT).
  4. If the item has nutrition data, the recipe's nutrition totals update automatically.

docs.title: 3. Preparation Steps
docs.text:
1. Add steps with markdown instructions and optional time (e.g., "knead for 10 minutes").
  2. Steps are displayed in order. Use drag-and-drop to reorder them.
  3. The new order is saved automatically.

docs.title: 4. Viewing Nutrition Data
docs.text:
• The recipe detail page shows total nutrition aggregated from all ingredients, grouped by nutrient type (e.g., calories, protein, fat).
  • Nutrition entries are flexible key-value-unit triples — you can track any nutrient (calories, protein, sodium, fiber, etc.).
  • Ingredients without nutrition entries are flagged so you know what's missing.
  • To add nutrition data to an item, go to the item in the shopping catalog and add entries like protein: 23.4 g or calories: 165 kcal.

docs.title: 5. Rating Recipes
docs.text:
• Click the stars on a recipe to rate it from 1 to 5.
  • The recipes list shows the average rating.
  • Expand the rating section to see who rated, what score, and when.

docs.title: 6. Adding Comments
docs.text:
• Scroll to the comments section on any recipe and add your thoughts, tips, or variations.
  • The recipe creator receives a notification when a new comment is added.

docs.title: 7. Configuring Meal Times
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Recipes & Meals Meal Times.
  2. Create meal times like Breakfast, Lunch, Dinner, or Snack.
  3. Set different times for each day of the week (e.g., Lunch at 12:00 on weekdays, 13:00 on weekends).

docs.title: 8. Planning Weekly Meals
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Recipes & Meals Meal Planner.
  2. Create a plan for a specific week (Monday–Sunday).
  3. For each meal time and day, assign one or more recipes.
  4. Assign recipes to specific members or to the entire household.
  5. Optionally set a reminder (e.g., 60 minutes before the meal).

docs.title: 9. Responding to Meal Plans
docs.text:
1. When a plan is published, you'll receive a notification.
  2. Review your assigned meals and either accept or suggest a change.
  3. If you suggest a different recipe, your assignment is automatically updated.

docs.title: 10. Thumbs Up/Down
docs.text:
• After a meal is marked as done, give it a or .
  • This helps the family track which meals everyone enjoyed.

docs.title: 11. Exporting to Shopping Lists
docs.text:
1. Open a meal plan and click Export to Shopping List.
  2. Choose to create a new list or add to an existing list.
  3. Review the merge preview — it shows new items and quantity increases for existing items.
  4. Confirm to apply. New items are added without a store assignment.

docs.title: 12. Notifications & Messages
docs.text:
• The bell icon in the header shows your unread notification count.
  • Click it to see notifications for meal plans, reminders, comments, and messages.
  • Use the Messages section to send direct messages to other household members.

docs.title: Rationale & Logic
docs.text:
Traceability

  • FR-23: Recipe Management: Core recipe CRUD with labels and metadata.
  • FR-31: Weekly Meal Plan: Monday–Sunday planner with multi-recipe meals.
  • FR-36: Shopping List Integration: Export with merge preview and confirmation.
  • FR-38: In-App Notifications: Notification bell with typed events.

Automatic Cleanup

Meal plans older than 10 weeks are automatically purged. Recipe labels with no associated recipes are deleted automatically.

docs.title: Shopping Assistant
docs.text: The Shopping module is the core collaborative feature of the Home Application, allowing your family to coordinate grocery runs, track prices, and manage digital loyalty assets.

docs.title: Core Features
docs.text:
• Shared Lists

    Create lists that are instantly visible to every member of your household. Changes sync in real-time.

  • Digital Cards

    Store your store loyalty numbers and render them as scannable barcodes directly on your phone.

  • Coupons

    Track store-specific coupons with expiration dates. Receive dashboard warnings for items expiring soon.

  • Smart Pricing

    The system remembers what you paid for items at different stores and suggests prices automatically.

docs.title: 1. Planning a Grocery Run
docs.text:
1. Navigate to Shopping Lists.
  2. Click New List and give it a name (e.g., "Weekly Groceries").
  3. Add items by selecting them from your Master Catalog.
  4. Specify the Store where you plan to buy each item to get price suggestions.

docs.title: 2. In-Store Experience
docs.text:
1. Open the list on your mobile device.
  2. Switch to Shopping Mode for an optimized, large-target interface.
  3. Tap an item to mark it as Bought. It will move to the bottom with a strikethrough.
  4. Access your Loyalty Cards directly from the store details to scan at the checkout.

docs.title: Rationale & Logic
docs.text:
Traceability

  • FR-7: Shopping List Collaboration: Powers real-time household synchronization.
  • FR-9: Price Suggestions: Logic for historical price tracking and estimates.
  • FR-12: Loyalty Cards: Barcode and QR code rendering for store scanners.

Automatic Cleanup

To keep your dashboard clean, completed lists and their items are automatically archived and purged after 3 months.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Home Application ecosystem is built using the Spec-Driven Development (SDD) methodology. This documentation-first approach ensures that all features are thoroughly reasoned, formally defined, and verifiable before a single line of code is produced.

For a detailed breakdown of our SDD Philosophy, Workflow, and Syntax Standards (EARS), please refer to the technical guidelines:

SDD Methodology & Standards

docs.title: Specification Structure
docs.text:
The project specification is organized into a hierarchical structure to ensure clarity and ease of navigation.

  • Requirements

    Functional (FR) and Non-Functional (NFR) requirements grouped by domain.

  • IconPencil Design

    High-level architecture, database schema, and module-specific implementation details.

  • Test Strategy

    Verification plans, test pyramid definition, and linkable test scenarios.

  • Roadmap

    Sequential feature rollout and task-level execution plans.

docs.title: SDD Lifecycle
docs.text:
The following phases define the transactional lifecycle of every feature in the system:

  1. Init: Establish the structure and inspect the baseline.
  2. Requirements: Define exactly What needs to be built.
  3. Design: Plan How it will be implemented and verified.
  4. Tasking: Break down the design into transactional units of work.
  5. Execution: Implement and validate against the spec.

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: The Home Application is a household management platform with a Spring Boot backend and React frontend. It provides modular features for family members to collaborate on shared resources including shopping, recipes, meal planning, and notifications.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
• Collaborative real-time updates via TanStack Query
  • Security (Zero Trust) with Google OAuth2
  • Modular navigation with nested menus

docs.title: Architecture at a Glance
docs.text:
graph TD
    Frontend["React 19 + Mantine 7"]
    Backend["Spring Boot 4.0 + PostgreSQL"]
    Auth["Google OAuth2"]

    Frontend -->|REST/HAL+JSON| Backend
    Frontend -->|OAuth2| Auth
    Backend -->|JDBC| PostgreSQL

docs.title: 2. Tech Stack
docs.text: BackendFrontendTestingBuild Framework Role Spring Boot 4.0 Core backend framework for DI, Security, and REST. Spring Security OAuth2 Client and session management. Spring HATEOAS REST API with hypermedia controls (HAL format). Spring Data JPA Database access and repository pattern. OpenFeign Declarative HTTP client for Google People API integration. Liquibase DB schema versioning and migration management. Framework Role React 19 Core frontend library with Concurrent Mode features. Mantine 7 UI component library for consistent and accessible design. TanStack Query v5 Server state management, caching, and optimistic updates. @tabler/icons-react SVG icon system for UI elements. react-barcode Generates Code 128 barcodes for loyalty cards. qrcode.react Generates QR codes for loyalty cards. Framework Role Vitest Fast unit test runner for frontend with jsdom environment. Playwright E2E testing framework with MSW browser mocks. MSW API mocking for both unit and E2E tests. Spock Backend integration testing with Groovy. Testcontainers PostgreSQL containers for integration tests. Framework Role MkDocs Documentation generator using Markdown. Material for MkDocs Professional theme for documentation site. ReDoc API documentation from OpenAPI 3.0 specifications. Gradle Build automation and dependency management.

docs.title: Frontend Architecture
docs.text:
The frontend module is built with React 19 and Mantine 7, following a modern component-driven architecture.

It uses TanStack Query v5 for server state management, enabling efficient data fetching, caching, and optimistic updates.

The application is organized into a clear separation of concerns: components/ for reusable UI elements, pages/ for route-level components, services/ for API client logic, and hooks/ for custom React hooks.

State is persisted via localStorage for UI preferences like sidebar expansion state.

graph TD
    App["App.tsx\nProviders + Router"]
    Layout["Layout.tsx\nMain wrapper"]
    Sidebar["Sidebar.tsx\nNavigation"]
    Header["Header.tsx\nUser menu"]
    Common["common/\nShared UI"]
    Dashboard["Dashboard"]
    Shopping["Shopping\nLists/Stores/Coupons"]
    Settings["Settings\nFamily/Roles"]

    Settings --> API

Frontend Design

docs.title: Backend Architecture
docs.text:
The backend module is built with Spring Boot 4.0 and Java 25, following a layered architecture pattern.

It uses Spring Security for OAuth2 authentication and Spring HATEOAS for REST API responses with hypermedia controls.

The application is organized into clear layers: controller/ for REST endpoints, service/ for business logic, repository/ for data access, and config/ for application configuration.

Scheduled tasks handle data retention and age group recalculation, while Testcontainers and WireMock enable comprehensive integration testing.

graph TD
