    Ctrl["REST Controllers\nHATEOAS"]
    Srv["Services\nTransactional"]
    Repo["Repositories\nJPA"]
    DB[(PostgreSQL)]
    Feign["OpenFeign\nGoogle People API"]
    Sched["Scheduled Tasks\nRetention/Age"]
    Auth["Spring Security\nOAuth2"]

    API --> Auth
    Auth --> Ctrl
    Ctrl --> Srv
    Srv --> Repo
    Repo --> DB
    Srv --> Feign
    Sched --> Srv

Backend Design

docs.title: Database Schema
docs.text:
The database uses PostgreSQL 17+ with six logical schemas: profiles for user and authentication data, shopping for shopping-related data, recipes for the family cookbook and nutrition, meals for weekly meal planning, notifications for in-app notifications and messaging, and media for centralized binary photo storage.

This separation provides clear domain boundaries and simplifies access control.

The profiles schema manages user accounts, extended profile data, family roles, and age group configurations.

The shopping schema handles shopping lists, items (with unit, piece conversion, and nutrition sample config), categories, stores, loyalty cards, and coupons (with optional barcode support).

The recipes schema manages recipes, photos (referencing centralized media by name), dynamic labels, ingredients (linked to shopping items with optional grouping), preparation steps, comments, ratings, a nutrients master catalog, and per-item nutrition data.

The meals schema handles meal time configuration with per-day schedules and sort order, weekly meal plans (Monday–Sunday), multi-recipe meal entries with multipliers, standalone item assignments, and thumbs up/down votes.

The notifications schema manages typed in-app notifications with link-based navigation and sender attribution, plus direct user-to-user messaging.

The media schema provides centralized binary photo storage (BYTEA) served via /api/images/{name}, used by all modules for profile, recipe, item, and store photos.

All tables include audit columns (created_at, updated_at, version) for optimistic locking and data tracking.

graph TD
    subgraph profiles
        users["users\nAuthentication"]
        user_profiles["user_profiles\nProfile data"]
        family_roles["family_roles\nRoles"]
        age_group_config["age_group_config\nAge ranges"]
    end

    subgraph shopping

docs.title: API Design
docs.text:
The API follows a RESTful design with HATEOAS (Hypermedia as the Engine of Application State) for discoverability.

All responses return application/hal+json with _links for navigation. Authentication is handled via Google OAuth2 access tokens in the Authorization: Bearer header.

The API contract is defined in an OpenAPI 3.0 specification (docs/specification/design/backend/api/openapi.yaml) and rendered interactively using ReDoc. Endpoints are organized by domain: User Profiles, Settings (Adults only), and Shopping (Lists, Items, Stores, Coupons).

graph TD
    Client["API Client\nfetch"]
    Auth["Spring Security\nOAuth2"]
    Ctrl["REST Controllers\nHATEOAS"]
    Srv["Services\nTransactional"]
    Repo["Repositories\nJPA"]
    DB[(PostgreSQL)]

    Client -->|Bearer Token| Auth
    Auth --> Ctrl
    Ctrl --> Srv
    Srv --> Repo
    Repo --> DB

    subgraph OpenAPI
        OAS["docs/specification/design/backend/api/openapi.yaml"]
        ReDoc["ReDoc\nInteractive Docs"]
    end

    OAS --> ReDoc
API Reference

docs.title: 4. Key Design Decisions
docs.text: Decision Status Rationale HATEOAS for REST API Accepted Discoverable APIs, better client integration Monorepo with Gradle Accepted Shared tooling, atomic changes PostgreSQL with schemas Accepted Clear data separation Physical deletion (no soft delete) Accepted Simpler queries, FR-11 retention policy Multi-recipe meals Accepted A meal entry can combine multiple recipes (e.g., protein + salad) Dynamic labels with auto-cleanup Accepted Labels created on demand, deleted when orphaned. No predefined set On-the-fly nutrition calculation Accepted Recipe nutrition totals computed at query time, not stored. Always accurate Centralized media service Accepted All photos stored in media.photos (BYTEA), served via /api/images/{name}. Replaces per-table Base64 Predefined nutrient catalog Accepted Nutrition entries reference a managed recipes.nutrients master table instead of free-form key-value-unit

docs.title: Backend
docs.text:
NFR-2: Performance (Latency)

Target: 95% of requests < 150ms. Core CRUD operations MUST be highly optimized.

  • Target: 95% of requests < 150ms
  • Indexes: Optimized for price suggestions and coupon queries
  • Retention Task (Shopping): Daily at 02:00 AM ( FR-11)
  • Retention Task (Meals): Daily at 03:00 AM ( FR-34)
  • Age Recalculation: Daily at 00:01 AM ( FR-16)
  • Meal Reminder Check: Every 15 minutes ( FR-33) — Deferred, not yet functional

docs.title: Frontend
docs.text:
NFR-3: Reliability & Sync

Mobile UI SHALL support offline viewing and checking of items with automatic synchronization.

Caching Strategy

  • Stale-While-Revalidate: staleTime: 300000 (5 minutes)
  • Optimistic Sync: Immediate local cache updates

docs.title: 6. Error Handling
docs.text:
All errors follow RFC 7807 (Problem Detail) format.

See API Reference for:

  • Validation error examples
  • HTTP status codes (401, 403, 503)
  • Problem Detail JSON structure

docs.title: Example Problem Detail Payload
docs.text:
{
  "type": "https://api.homeapp.com/problems/validation-error",
  "title": "Validation Failed",
  "status": 400,
  "detail": "One or more fields failed validation",
  "instance": "/api/user/me",
  "errors": {
    "mobilePhone": "Invalid format. Must be 7-20 digits with optional + prefix.",
    "facebook": "Must be a valid Facebook profile URL."
  }
}

docs.text:

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Home Application backend is a robust Spring Boot 4.0 service built with Java 25. It provides a secure, hypermedia-driven REST API, handles background automation, and manages complex family hierarchies and data retention policies.

  • Hypermedia-Driven

    Implements HATEOAS with HAL-formatted JSON, making the API discoverable and decoupled from the client.

  • OAuth2 Secure

    Integrates Spring Security with Google OAuth2 for identity and session management, including cookie-based CSRF protection.

  • Automated Tasks

    Built-in scheduling for critical background logic like age recalculation and automatic data retention.

docs.title: Layered Architecture
docs.text:
The backend follows a standard layered architecture to ensure separation of concerns and maintainability.

graph TD
    Ctrl["Controllers\n(HATEOAS)"]
    Srv["Services\nTransactional"]
    Repo["Repositories\nJPA"]
    DB[(PostgreSQL)]

    Ctrl --> Srv
    Srv --> Repo
    Repo --> DB

docs.title: Package Structure
docs.text:
com.jorgemonteiro.home_app/
├── controller.<schema>/    # REST Endpoints
├── service.<schema>/       # Business Logic
├── repository.<schema>/    # Data Access (JPA)
├── model/
│   ├── entities/           # DB Entities
│   ├── dtos/               # API Data Objects
│   └── adapter/            # Mappers (Entity ↔ DTO)
└── config/                 # Security & Beans

Schemas: profiles, shopping, recipes, meals, notifications, media.

docs.title: Google OAuth2 Integration
docs.text:
By leveraging Google OAuth2, the application offloads credential management while benefiting from Google's security features (MFA, phishing protection).

graph TD
    Google["Google OAuth2\nAuthorization Server"]
    Security["Spring Security\nOAuth2 Client"]
    UserDetailsService["Custom UserDetailsService"]
    Controllers["REST Controllers\n(HATEOAS)"]
    Services["Services\nTransactional"]
    Repositories["Repositories\nJPA"]
    DB[(PostgreSQL)]

    Google -->|User Info| Security
    Security -->|Load User| UserDetailsService
    UserDetailsService -->|Session| Security
    Security -->|Authenticated| Controllers
    Controllers --> Services
    Services --> Repositories
    Repositories --> DB

Access Control

All API endpoints require a valid OAuth2 session cookie. Non-authenticated requests to /api/** return 401 Unauthorized.

docs.title: Scheduled Tasks
docs.text: Spring Boot provides built-in scheduling support via @Scheduled.

docs.title: Data Retention (Shopping)
docs.text:
FR-11: Automatic Data Retention

System MUST identify and permanently delete shopping lists and items older than 3 months.

Runs daily at 02:00 AM.

@Scheduled(cron = "0 0 2 * * ?")
@Transactional
public void purgeOldLists() {
    LocalDateTime threshold = LocalDateTime.now().minusMonths(3);
    shoppingListRepository.deleteByCompletedAtBefore(threshold);
}

docs.title: Data Retention (Meals)
docs.text:
FR-34: Meal Plan Data Retention

System MUST permanently delete meal plans older than 10 weeks, including all associated entries, recipes, and member records.

Runs daily at 03:00 AM.

@Scheduled(cron = "0 0 3 * * ?")
@Transactional
public void purgeOldMealPlans() {
    LocalDate threshold = LocalDate.now().minusWeeks(10);
    mealPlanRepository.deleteByWeekStartDateBefore(threshold);
}

docs.title: Meal Reminder Check
docs.text:
FR-33: Meal Preparation Reminders

System checks for upcoming meals with configured reminders and creates MEAL_REMINDER notifications.

Deferred

This scheduler is scaffolded but not yet functional. The reminder_offset_minutes column is not yet in the database schema.

Runs every 15 minutes. Queries meal_plan_entries with reminder_offset_minutes set, creates notifications for meals where (meal_time - reminder_offset) falls within the next 15-minute window.

@Scheduled(cron = "0 */15 * * * ?")
@Transactional
public void checkMealReminders() {
    // TODO: Not yet functional - schema column pending
}

docs.title: Age Recalculation
docs.text:
FR-16: Automated Age Group Classification

System calculates the user's age based on birthdate and maps it to the configured Age Groups (Child, Teenager, Adult).

Runs daily at 00:01 AM or upon user login.

docs.title: Performance & Optimization
docs.text:
NFR-2: Performance (Latency)

Target: 95% of requests < 150ms. Core CRUD operations MUST be highly optimized.

Optimized Indexes: - users(email): Unique lookup for auth. - shopping_list_items(item_id, store_id, created_at DESC): Rapid price suggestions. - shopping_coupons(used, due_date): Efficient dashboard widgets. - recipe_ratings(recipe_id): Fast average rating calculation. - recipe_ingredients(recipe_id): Efficient ingredient listing. - recipe_steps(recipe_id, sort_order): Ordered step retrieval. - meal_plans(week_start_date): Quick week lookup. - meal_plan_entries(meal_plan_id): Efficient plan loading. - notifications(user_id, read, created_at DESC): Fast unread notification queries.

docs.title: Testing Strategy
docs.text:
The backend uses Spock Framework (Groovy) for all tests, with Testcontainers providing real PostgreSQL instances.

Layer Purpose Framework Unit Service & Adapter logic Spock Integration Full API tests with real DB Spock + Testcontainers Security OAuth2 & RBAC validation Spring Security Test External Google People API simulation WireMock

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Home Application API is a RESTful service built with Spring Boot, following HATEOAS principles and using HAL (Hypermedia Application Language) for resource representation.

docs.title: API Reference
docs.text:
• Complete endpoint documentation
  • :material-schema: Request/response schemas
  • Interactive testing console (via ReDoc)

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text:
FR-1: Google OAuth2 Authentication

The system SHALL redirect the user to Google's auth provider and process the authorization code.

NFR-1: Security (Zero Trust)

Any request to /api/** without a valid session SHALL return 401 Unauthorized.

The API is secured using Google OAuth2. All requests to /api/** (except the login redirect) MUST include a valid Bearer token.

Header: 

Authorization: Bearer {google_access_token}

  • Session Management: Spring Security handles the OAuth2 flow and establishes a session.
  • CSRF Protection: Mutating requests (POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) require a valid X-XSRF-TOKEN header, which must match the value in the XSRF-TOKEN cookie.

docs.title: Error Handling (RFC 7807)
docs.text:
The API uses the Problem Detail for HTTP APIs (RFC 7807) standard for all error responses. This provides a consistent, machine-readable format for both validation and system errors.

Sample Problem Detail Payload:

{
  "type": "https://home-app.dev/errors/validation-failed",
  "title": "Validation Failed",
  "status": 400,
  "detail": "One or more fields failed validation",
  "instance": "/api/user/me",
  "errors": {
    "mobilePhone": "Must be a valid international phone number format.",
    "facebook": "Must be a valid Facebook profile URL."
  }
}

docs.title: Hypermedia (HATEOAS)
docs.text:
Resources use the application/hal+json content type and include a _links object to provide discoverability. Clients should use these links rather than hardcoding URL patterns.

Example Resource:

{
  "id": 42,
  "name": "Weekly Groceries",
  "status": "ACTIVE",
  "_links": {
    "self": { "href": "http://localhost:8080/api/shopping/lists/42" },
    "items": { "href": "http://localhost:8080/api/shopping/lists/42/items" },
    "complete": { "href": "http://localhost:8080/api/shopping/lists/42/status" }
  }
}

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Home Application uses PostgreSQL 17 as its primary data store. The database is organized into five distinct logical schemas to separate domain concerns.

  • Multi-Schema Design

    Uses profiles, shopping, recipes, meals, notifications, and media schemas to enforce domain boundaries and simplify access control.

  • Audit Tracking

    All tables include mandatory audit columns (created_at, updated_at, version) for tracking and optimistic locking.

  • Liquibase Managed

    Database migrations are versioned and managed via Liquibase, ensuring consistent schema state across environments.

docs.title: 1. Profiles Schema
docs.text:
Manages identity, authentication, and the household organizational structure.

Entity Description Users Core authentication record linked to Google email. User Profiles Extended profile data (birthdate, social links, photo URL). User Preferences UI-specific settings like dashboard widget visibility. Family Roles Defined roles (Mother, Father, etc.) with custom support. Age Groups Configurable age ranges for classification logic.

docs.title: 2. Shopping Schema
docs.text:
Contains all data related to the collaborative shopping experience.

Entity Description Shopping Lists Shared lists with status tracking (PENDING/COMPLETED). Shopping Items Master catalog items with unit, piece conversion, and nutrition sample config. Shopping List Items Individual list entries with bought/unavailable status. Stores Favorite shopping locations with associated metadata. Loyalty Cards Digital cards with QR/Code 128 barcode support. Coupons Store discounts with expiration tracking and optional barcode. Price History Historical price data for intelligent suggestions.

docs.title: 3. Recipes Schema
docs.text:
Manages the family cookbook and nutritional information.

Entity Description Recipes Core recipe records with markdown descriptions and metadata. Recipe Photos Photos referencing the centralized media service by photo_name. Labels Dynamic tags created on demand, auto-deleted when orphaned. Nutrients Master catalog of predefined nutrients (Energy, Fat, Protein, etc.). Ingredients Links to shopping items with quantity, unit, and optional group_name. Nutrition Entries Per-item nutrition values referencing the nutrients master catalog. Steps Ordered preparation instructions with optional time. Comments & Ratings Collaborative feedback: comments and 1-5 star ratings.

docs.title: 4. Meals Schema
docs.text:
Handles weekly meal planning and feedback.

Entity Description Meal Times Named meal occasions with per-day-of-week schedules and sort order. Meal Plans Weekly plans (Monday–Sunday) with PENDING/PUBLISHED status. Entries Individual meal slots with done flag. Entry Recipes Recipe assignments per entry with multiplier, optionally per member. Entry Items Standalone shopping item assignments per entry, optionally per member. Votes Thumbs up/down feedback per entry per user.

docs.title: 5. Notifications Schema
docs.text:
Manages in-app notifications and direct messaging.

Entity Description Notifications Typed events with link for navigation and sender info. Messages Direct messages between household members with read tracking.

docs.title: 6. Media Schema
docs.text:
Centralized binary photo storage serving all modules.

Entity Description Photos Binary image data (BYTEA) with name, type, extension, and content type.

docs.title: Retention Policy
docs.text:
FR-11: Automatic Data Retention

To maintain system performance and declutter the UI, the system automatically purges completed shopping lists and associated items older than 3 months.

FR-34: Meal Plan Data Retention

Meal plans with a week_start_date older than 10 weeks are permanently deleted by a daily scheduled task at 03:00 AM, including all associated entries, recipes, and member records.

docs.title: Label Auto-Cleanup
docs.text:
FR-23: Dynamic Labels

Labels are managed at the application level, not by a scheduled task. When a label is removed from a recipe and no other recipe references it, the service layer deletes the orphaned label immediately.

docs.title: Integrity & Security
docs.text:
• Optimistic Locking: The version column prevents lost updates during concurrent edits.
  • Physical Deletion: Data retention is handled by physical DELETE operations to keep the database lean.
  • Schema Validation: The application performs strict schema validation on startup via spring.jpa.hibernate.ddl-auto: validate.

docs.title: Table Definitions
docs.text: Table Description recipes Core recipe records with metadata, markdown description, and creator reference. Unique constraint on (name, created_by). recipe_photos Photos referencing the centralized media service by photo_name. One can be designated as default via is_default boolean. labels Dynamic label catalog. Created on demand, auto-deleted when no recipe references them. recipe_labels Junction table linking recipes to labels (many-to-many). nutrients Master catalog of predefined nutrients (Energy, Fat, Protein, etc.) with name, description, and unit. recipe_ingredients Links shopping items as recipe ingredients with quantity, unit (same enum as shopping: KG, G, L, ML, PACK, UNIT), and optional group_name for visual grouping. recipe_steps Ordered preparation steps with markdown instructions and optional time. sort_order determines display order. recipe_comments User comments on recipes with author and timestamp. Column: comment. recipe_ratings Individual 1-5 star ratings per user per recipe (unique constraint on recipe_id + user_id). Column: rating. Average computed on-the-fly. nutrition_entries Nutrition data per shopping item, referencing the nutrients master table via nutrient_id FK. Unique constraint on (item_id, nutrient_id).

docs.text: Table Description meal_times Named meal occasions (e.g., Breakfast, Lunch, Dinner) with sort_order for display ordering. meal_time_schedules Per-day-of-week time configuration for each meal time. day_of_week uses 0-6 (0=Monday, 6=Sunday). Column: start_time. meal_plans Weekly plans with a unique week_start_date (Monday). Status: PENDING, PUBLISHED, ACCEPTED, CHANGED. meal_plan_entries Individual meal slots: a specific meal time on a specific day. Supports is_done flag. meal_plan_entry_recipes Recipes assigned to an entry with multiplier (default 1.0). user_id is nullable — null means "for everyone". Supports multi-recipe meals. meal_plan_entry_items Standalone shopping items assigned to an entry with quantity and unit. user_id is nullable — null means "for everyone". meal_plan_votes Thumbs up/down feedback per entry per user. Unique constraint on (entry_id, user_id). vote boolean: true = thumbs-up, false = thumbs-down.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Home Application frontend is a modern, responsive Single Page Application (SPA) built with React 19 and Mantine 7. It prioritizes a seamless user experience with real-time updates, offline capabilities, and a modular navigation system.

  • Component-Driven

    Built with Mantine 7, ensuring a consistent design system, high accessibility, and a mobile-first responsive layout.

  • State Management

    Powered by TanStack Query v5 for efficient server-state synchronization, caching, and optimistic UI updates.

  • Mobile Optimized

    Optimized for one-handed store use with large tap targets and a collapsible, persistent navigation sidebar.

docs.title: Tech Stack
docs.text:
• React 19

    Leverages Concurrent Mode and improved transitions for a fluid UI.

  • Mantine 7

    Core UI library providing accessible components, hooks, and a robust theme system.

  • TanStack Query

    Handles asynchronous data fetching, automatic refetching, and local cache management.

  • Tabler Icons

    Comprehensive SVG icon system via @tabler/icons-react.

docs.title: UI Architecture
docs.text:
• Component Hierarchy: Clear separation between Layout, Sidebar, and Feature Pages.
  • Navigation: Persistent sidebar with nested modules and state tracking.
  • Loyalty & Coupons: Specialized rendering for Barcodes (Code 128) and QR Codes.
  • Forms: Type-safe form management with validation via @mantine/form.

docs.title: Requirements Traceability
docs.text:
Implementation Links

  • FR-14: Module Navigation: Nested menus and sidebar persistence.
  • NFR-4: Accessibility & UX: Mobile-first design and 44x44px tap targets.
  • NFR-3: Reliability & Sync: Offline support and automatic synchronization.
  • FR-23: Recipe Management: Recipe CRUD with markdown, labels, and photos.
  • FR-31: Weekly Meal Plan: Meal planner with multi-recipe meals and member assignments.
  • FR-38: In-App Notifications: Notification bell in header with unread count.

docs.title: State Management
docs.text: The application leverages TanStack Query v5 to manage all asynchronous server state. This strategy minimizes unnecessary network traffic and ensures a highly responsive user interface.

docs.title: Server State & Caching
docs.text:
The "Server Cache" acts as the source of truth for all domain data. By configuring granular caching policies, the frontend achieves near-instant navigation between views.

NFR-2: Performance (Latency)

To meet the 150ms latency target, reference data (Categories, Stores) is cached with a long staleTime, while volatile data (Shopping Lists) uses aggressive invalidation.

Global Query Defaults: - staleTime: 5 * 60 * 1000: Data is considered "fresh" for 5 minutes. - gcTime: 10 * 60 * 1000: Unused data is kept in memory for 10 minutes before garbage collection. - retry: 1: Failed requests are retried once before showing an error UI.

Example: Cached Store Data 

export const useStores = () => {
  return useQuery({
    queryKey: ['stores'],
    queryFn: () => api.get<Store[]>('/api/shopping/stores'),
    staleTime: 10 * 60 * 1000, // Stores change rarely (fresh for 10m)
  });
};

docs.title: Data Synchronization & Mutations
docs.text:
Mutations handle all data-modifying operations (POST, PUT, DELETE). To ensure the UI reflects changes immediately, the application employs two primary strategies: Invalidation and Optimistic Updates.

NFR-3: Reliability & Sync

Mutations implement Optimistic Updates to ensure the UI remains responsive and functional even under high latency or intermittent connectivity.

docs.title: Pattern: Optimistic Update (Item Check-off)
docs.text:
When a user checks off an item, the UI is updated immediately before the server responds. If the server request fails, the UI is rolled back to the previous state.

export const useCheckItem = (listId: number) => {
  const queryClient = useQueryClient();

  return useMutation({
    mutationFn: (itemId: number) => api.patch(`/api/shopping/lists/${listId}/items/${itemId}/toggle`),

    // Step 1: Cancel outgoing fetches
    onMutate: async (itemId) => {
      await queryClient.cancelQueries({ queryKey: ['shopping-list', listId] });
      const previousList = queryClient.getQueryData(['shopping-list', listId]);

      // Step 2: Optimistically update the cache
      queryClient.setQueryData(['shopping-list', listId], (old: ShoppingList) => ({
        ...old,
        items: old.items.map(i => i.id === itemId ? { ...i, bought: !i.bought } : i)
      }));

      return { previousList }; // Return context for rollback
    },

    // Step 3: Rollback on error
    onError: (err, itemId, context) => {
      queryClient.setQueryData(['shopping-list', listId], context?.previousList);
      notifications.show({ color: 'red', message: 'Failed to sync update' });
    },

    // Step 4: Always refetch after error or success to sync with server
    onSettled: () => {
      queryClient.invalidateQueries({ queryKey: ['shopping-list', listId] });
    },
  });
};

docs.title: Modular Sidebar
docs.text:
FR-14: Module Navigation

The application implements a persistent, nested navigation system that adapts to user roles and persists its state across sessions.

  • Persistence: The expanded/collapsed state of parent menus (Shopping, Settings) is persisted in localStorage.
  • RBAC: Navigation items are conditionally rendered based on the user's Age Group (e.g., Settings is hidden for "Child" or "Teenager").

docs.title: User Profile Quick View
docs.text:
FR-3: User Profile Quick View

The header dropdown provides an immediate summary of the user's identity, including dynamic social links and role status.

  • Rendered as a dropdown in the Header.
  • Displays dynamic icons for LinkedIn, Facebook, and Instagram based on the presence of profile URLs.
  • Provides a direct link to the full Profile Update page.

docs.title: Barcode Generation
docs.text:
FR-12: Loyalty Cards

Digital loyalty cards are rendered as industry-standard linear barcodes (Code 128) or QR codes to ensure compatibility with physical store scanners.

docs.title: Coupon Urgency Logic
docs.text:
FR-15: Expiration Warning Panel

The dashboard proactively highlights coupons nearing their expiration date using a time-based urgency calculation.

const isUrgent = (dueDate: string): boolean => {
  const daysRemaining = differenceInDays(new Date(dueDate), new Date());
  return daysRemaining <= 3; // Highlight within 3 days of expiry
};

docs.title: Form Management
docs.text:
• All user inputs are handled via @mantine/form.
  • Validation: Synchronous client-side validation for phone formats and URL patterns (matching backend regex).

docs.title: Build & Quality
docs.text:
• Bundler: Vite 6 for rapid HMR (Hot Module Replacement).
  • Gradle Integration: The frontend build is wrapped in a Gradle task (:home-app-frontend:build) to ensure it runs during CI.
  • Linting: Strict ESLint configuration using eslint-plugin-react-refresh.

docs.title: Recipe Step Drag-and-Drop
docs.text:
FR-27: Preparation Steps

Recipe steps support drag-and-drop reordering via @dnd-kit/core. When a user drops a step in a new position, the UI sends a reorder request with the new step ID sequence.

docs.title: Markdown Editing
docs.text: Recipe descriptions and step instructions support markdown content. The existing MarkdownContent.tsx component handles rendering. For editing, a textarea with markdown preview toggle is used.

docs.title: Notification Bell
docs.text:
FR-38: In-App Notifications

The Header.tsx component includes a NotificationBell that displays an unread count badge. Clicking opens a dropdown with recent notifications. The count is polled via TanStack Query.

docs.text:
FR-35: Meals This Week

The Dashboard includes a "Meals This Week" card showing today's planned meals with recipe names and assigned members. A "View Full Week" link navigates to the dedicated MealsThisWeekPage.

The sidebar adds a Recipes & Meals parent menu with sub-items:

  • Meal Planner

And a Settings parent menu with sub-items:

Notifications are accessed via the bell icon in the header, not the sidebar.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: The Home Application follows a robust testing strategy designed to ensure reliability, security, and performance across both frontend and backend modules.

docs.title: Test Pyramid
docs.text:
The project adheres to a balanced testing strategy, prioritizing backend unit and integration tests while maintaining high-confidence frontend E2E suites.

graph TD
    subgraph Pyramid
        E2E["<b>Frontend E2E Tests</b><br/>Playwright + MSW<br/>(Confidence)"]
        INT["<b>Backend Integration Tests</b><br/>Spock + Testcontainers<br/>(Reliability)"]
        UNIT["<b>Backend Unit Tests</b><br/>Spock<br/>(Speed & Logic)"]
    end

    E2E --- INT
    INT --- UNIT

    style E2E fill:#f9f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style INT fill:#bbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

docs.title: Test Frameworks and Tools
docs.text: BackendFrontend Name Description / Purpose Spock Behavior-driven development (BDD) testing framework for Java and Groovy applications. Testcontainers Provides lightweight, throwaway instances of PostgreSQL 17 for integration tests. WireMock Mocks external HTTP-based services like the Google People API. JaCoCo Generates comprehensive code coverage reports for Java and Groovy code. Spring Security Test Utilities for testing Spring Security configurations and role-based access control. Name Description / Purpose Vitest Fast unit test runner for frontend with jsdom environment. Playwright Framework for reliable end-to-end testing across modern web browsers. MSW Mock Service Worker for API mocking in both unit and E2E tests.

docs.title: 1. Unit Tests (Base)
docs.text:
• Scope: Individual backend services and utility functions.
  • Backend: Spock for business logic and adapter transformations.
  • Goal: Exhaustive coverage of edge cases and business rules.

docs.title: 2. Integration Tests (Middle)
docs.text:
• Scope: API endpoints, database interactions, and external service contracts.
  • Tools: Spock + Testcontainers (PostgreSQL 17).
  • External APIs: WireMock for Google People API simulation.
  • Goal: Verify data persistence, security constraints (RBAC), and transactional integrity.

docs.title: 3. E2E Tests (Top)
docs.text:
• Scope: Complete user journeys across the UI.
  • Tools: Playwright with MSW (Mock Service Worker) for controlled state.
  • Goal: Validate critical paths like onboarding, collaborative shopping, and role-based access.

docs.title: Coverage & Quality Targets
docs.text:
NFR-2: Performance (Latency)

Performance tests MUST ensure that 95% of API requests complete in < 150ms.

Metric Target Tool Backend Code Coverage > 80% (Lines) JaCoCo Build Stability 100% Success GitHub Actions API Latency < 150ms (p95) Custom Metrics Security 0 Critical Vulnerabilities Dependency Check

docs.title: CI/CD Pipeline
docs.text: Tests are executed automatically on every Pull Request via GitHub Actions. A change is only considered "Ready for Review" once all suites pass and coverage targets are met.

docs.title: Test Scenarios
docs.text:
Legend

  • Positive Case: Verifies expected behavior under normal conditions.
  • Negative Case: Verifies system resilience and error handling under failure or invalid conditions.

docs.title: TS-1: Google OAuth2 User Registration
docs.text:
• Given: A user who has never logged in to the application.
  • When: The user successfully authenticates via Google OAuth2.
  • Then: A new local user record and profile are created.

Validates

  • FR-1: Google OAuth2 Authentication
  • FR-2: Automatic User Registration

docs.title: TS-2: Google OAuth2 Login (Returning User)
docs.text:
• Given: A user who already has a local account.
  • When: The user successfully authenticates via Google OAuth2.
  • Then: The existing local user record is retrieved.

Validates

  • FR-1: Google OAuth2 Authentication

docs.title: TS-3: Authentication Failure
docs.text:
• Given: A user who cancels the login process at Google.
  • When: The user is redirected back to the application.
  • Then: The system redirects them to /login?error=true.

Validates

  • FR-1: Google OAuth2 Authentication

docs.title: TS-4: Service Timeout / Token Exchange Failure
docs.text:
• Given: An issue with Google's token endpoint.
  • When: The backend attempts to exchange the authorization code for a token.
  • Then: The system displays a "Service Unavailable" error message.

Validates

  • FR-1: Google OAuth2 Authentication

docs.title: TS-7: Successful User Profile Update
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user.
  • When: The user sends a PUT /api/user/me with valid social links, a phone number, and a new photo.
  • Then: The system updates the profile, returns 200 OK with the updated HATEOAS resource, and displays a success notification (toast) in the UI.

Validates

  • FR-4: Update User Profile

docs.title: TS-8: Profile Update Validation Failure
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user.
  • When: The user sends a PUT /api/user/me with an invalid phone format or a malformed Facebook URL.
  • Then: The system returns 400 Bad Request with a list of field-level violations.

Validates

  • FR-4: Update User Profile

docs.title: TS-9: Protection of Immutable Fields
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user.
  • When: The user sends a PUT /api/user/me containing a new firstName or email.
  • Then: The system updates the allowed profile fields but ignores the changes to firstName and email.

Validates

  • FR-4: Update User Profile

docs.title: TS-10: Profile Dropdown Detailed View (Conditional)
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user with a mobile phone and a LinkedIn profile.
  • When: The user opens the header dropdown menu.
  • Then: The system displays the mobile phone and a clickable LinkedIn icon/link.

Validates

  • FR-3: User Profile Quick View

docs.title: TS-11: Profile Dropdown Compact View (Fallback)
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user with no optional profile fields (phone, social).
  • When: The user opens the header dropdown menu.
  • Then: The system displays only basic info (name, photo) and the "View/Edit Profile" link, omitting phone and social sections.

Validates

  • FR-3: User Profile Quick View

docs.title: TS-23: Category & Master Item Creation
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user.
  • When: The user creates a "Dairy" category and then adds a "Whole Milk" item linked to it.
  • Then: The system SHALL persist the category and the item, ensuring the link is established.

Validates

  • FR-5: Category & Item Management

docs.title: TS-30: Store Management with Photos
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user.
  • When: The user creates a new Store with a name, description, and an uploaded photo.
  • Then: The system SHALL save the store details and correctly display the photo in the list.

Validates

  • FR-6: Store Management

docs.title: TS-31: Shopping List Visibility & Collaboration
docs.text:
• Given: User A creates a "Weekend Party" shopping list.
  • When: User B logs in.
  • Then: User B SHALL see the "Weekend Party" list on their dashboard and be able to add items to it.

Validates

  • FR-7: Shopping List Creation & Collaboration

docs.title: TS-13: Collaborative Item Check-off (Real-time)
docs.text:
• Given: Two users (A and B) belong to the same household and are viewing the same shopping list.
  • When: User A marks "Apples" as "Bought".
  • Then: The backend updates the item status, and User B's UI automatically reflects the "Bought" state.

Validates

  • FR-7: Shopping List Creation & Collaboration
  • FR-10: In-Store Progress Tracking

docs.title: TS-14: Intelligent Price Suggestions
docs.text:
• Given: A user has previously bought "Bread" at "Store X" for €1.50 and "Store Y" for €1.20.
  • When: The user adds "Bread" to a new list and selects "Store X".
  • Then: The system suggests €1.50 as the price.

Validates

  • FR-9: Price History & Suggestions

docs.title: TS-25: Global Price Suggestion Fallback
docs.text:
• Given: A user has previously bought "Bread" at "Store X" for €1.50 (most recent) and "Store Y" for €1.20.
  • When: The user adds "Bread" to a list and selects "Any Place".
  • Then: The system SHALL suggest €1.50 as the Global Last Price.

Validates

  • FR-9: Price History & Suggestions

docs.title: TS-15: Automatic Data Retention (Cleanup)
docs.text:
• Given: A shopping list that was marked as "Completed" 95 days ago.
  • When: The daily retention background task executes.
  • Then: The system SHALL permanently delete the list and all its associated ListItem records.

Validates

  • FR-11: Automatic Data Retention

docs.title: TS-17: Loyalty Card Barcode Validation
docs.text:
• Given: A loyalty card configured with the "Code 128" barcode type.
  • When: The user views the card details in the UI.
  • Then: The system SHALL render a linear 1D barcode encoding the exact card number.

Validates

  • FR-12: Loyalty Cards

docs.title: TS-24: Coupon Usage Tracking & Filtering
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user with an active coupon.
  • When: The user marks the coupon as "Used".
  • Then: The coupon SHALL be hidden from the default active view and the Dashboard warning panel.

Validates

  • FR-13: Store Coupons

docs.title: TS-16: Dashboard Coupon Warning
docs.text:
• Given: An unused coupon for "Lidl" expiring in 48 hours.
  • When: The user views the application Dashboard.
  • Then: The "Expiring Coupons" panel displays the Lidl coupon with its expiration date highlighted.

Validates

  • FR-15: Expiration Warning Panel (Dashboard)

docs.title: TS-18: First User Bootstrap (Onboarding)
docs.text:
• Given: A completely empty system with no users.
  • When: The first user successfully authenticates via Google.
  • Then: The system SHALL assign them the "Adult" age group, regardless of their actual age.

Validates

  • UJ-1: First-Time Login (Onboarding)

docs.title: TS-19: Automated Age Group Classification
docs.text:
• Given: Configured age ranges: Child (0-12), Teenager (13-17), Adult (18+).
  • And: A user with a birthdate exactly 13 years ago today.
  • When: The user logs in or the daily recalculation task runs.
  • Then: The user's profile SHALL be updated from "Child" to "Teenager".

Validates

  • FR-16: Automated Age Group Classification

docs.title: TS-20: Google Birthdate Synchronization
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated session with the user.birthday.read scope.
  • When: The onboarding process calls the Google People API.
  • Then: The system SHALL extract the birthdate and persist it in the UserProfile.
  • And: If Google returns no birthdate, the system SHALL prompt the user for manual entry.

Validates

  • FR-17: Google Birthdate Integration

docs.title: TS-28: Age Range Update & Global Recalculation
docs.text:
• Given: An "Adult" user.
  • When: The user modifies the "Teenager" range in the Settings module.
  • Then: The system SHALL trigger a recalculation of age groups for all household members based on the new ranges.

Validates

  • FR-18: Age Group Configuration
  • UJ-5: Configuring Household Age Groups

docs.title: TS-27: Age Range Contiguity & Overlap Validation
docs.text:
• Given: An "Adult" user.
  • When: The user attempts to save age ranges that overlap (e.g., Child 0-13, Teenager 12-18).
  • Then: The system SHALL return a validation error and prevent saving.

Validates

  • FR-18: Age Group Configuration

docs.title: TS-26: Custom Family Role Management
docs.text:
• Given: An "Adult" user.
  • When: The user creates a custom role "Grandmother" in the Settings module.
  • Then: The role SHALL become available for selection in user profiles.

Validates

  • FR-19: Family Role Configuration

docs.title: TS-21: Settings Access Control
docs.text:
• Given: A user classified as "Child" or "Teenager".
  • When: They attempt to access GET /api/settings/** or PUT /api/settings/**.
  • Then: The system SHALL return a 403 Forbidden status.

Validates

  • FR-18: Age Group Configuration
  • FR-19: Family Role Configuration

docs.title: TS-5: Database Offline
docs.text:
• Given: The PostgreSQL database is unreachable.
  • When: An API request is made to /api/user/me.
  • Then: The system returns a 503 Service Unavailable with an RFC 7807 Problem Detail.

Validates

  • NFR-3: Reliability & Sync

docs.title: TS-6: Unauthorized Access (Zero Trust)
docs.text:
• Given: An unauthenticated request.
  • When: Accessing /api/user/me.
  • Then: The system returns a 401 Unauthorized status.

Validates

  • NFR-1: Security (Zero Trust)

docs.title: TS-12: Frontend Cache Utilization
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user who has already loaded their profile.
  • When: The user navigates between the Dashboard and Profile pages within a 5-minute window.
  • Then: The system SHALL NOT trigger a new network request to /api/user/me, serving the data from the TanStack Query cache instead.

Validates

  • NFR-2: Performance (Latency)

docs.title: TS-22: Documentation Generation Verification
docs.text:
• Given: A valid mkdocs.yml and markdown files in docs/.
  • When: The Gradle task ./gradlew generateDocs is executed.
  • Then: A directory build/docs/ SHALL be created containing a functional static HTML site.

Validates

  • FR-20: Integrated Documentation Build (MkDocs)
  • FR-21: Gradle Integration

docs.title: TS-30B: Recipe CRUD with Photos
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user.
  • When: The user creates a recipe with a name, markdown description, two photos, and sets one as default.
  • Then: The system SHALL persist the recipe and photos, and the recipes list SHALL display the default photo.

Validates

  • FR-23: Recipe Management
  • FR-24: Recipe Photos

docs.title: TS-31B: Dynamic Label Lifecycle
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user creating a recipe.
  • When: The user types "Italian" (new label) and "Comfort Food" (existing label).
  • Then: The system SHALL create "Italian" in the labels table and link both labels to the recipe.
  • When: The user later removes "Italian" from the only recipe that uses it.
  • Then: The system SHALL delete "Italian" from the labels table.

Validates

  • FR-23: Recipe Management

docs.title: TS-32: Ingredient Linking with Unit Validation
docs.text:
• Given: A recipe and a shopping item "Chicken Breast" in the catalog.
  • When: The user adds "Chicken Breast" as an ingredient with quantity 500 and unit G.
  • Then: The system SHALL persist the ingredient with the same unit enum used by the Shopping module.

Validates

  • FR-25: Recipe Ingredients

docs.title: TS-33: Step Reordering
docs.text:
• Given: A recipe with 3 preparation steps in order [A, B, C].
  • When: The user drags step C to position 1.
  • Then: The system SHALL update sort_order values so the order becomes [C, A, B] in a single transaction.

Validates

  • FR-27: Preparation Steps

docs.title: TS-34: On-the-Fly Nutrition Calculation
docs.text:
• Given: A recipe with 2 ingredients: "Rice" (200g, nutrition entries: [calories: 130 kcal, protein: 2.7 g]) and "Chicken" (300g, nutrition entries: [calories: 165 kcal, protein: 31 g]).
  • When: The user views the recipe detail.
  • Then: The system SHALL display aggregated totals grouped by nutrient key: [calories: 295 kcal, protein: 14.7 g], computed on-the-fly.

Validates

  • FR-26: Nutrition Data

docs.title: TS-35: Nutrition Calculation with Missing Data
docs.text:
• Given: A recipe with 2 ingredients, one with nutrition entries and one without.
  • When: The user views the recipe detail.
  • Then: The system SHALL calculate totals from the ingredient with entries and indicate which ingredient is missing nutrition data.

Validates

  • FR-26: Nutrition Data

docs.title: TS-36: Rating Average with Individual Votes
docs.text:
• Given: User A rates a recipe 4 stars, User B rates it 5 stars.
  • When: Any user views the recipe.
  • Then: The system SHALL display an average of 4.5 and, when expanded, show User A (4 stars) and User B (5 stars) with timestamps.

Validates

  • FR-29: Recipe Ratings

docs.title: TS-37: Rating Uniqueness
docs.text:
• Given: User A has already rated a recipe 3 stars.
  • When: User A submits a new rating of 5 stars.
  • Then: The system SHALL update the existing rating to 5 (not create a duplicate).

Validates

  • FR-29: Recipe Ratings

docs.title: TS-38: Recipe Comment Notification
docs.text:
• Given: User A created a recipe.
  • When: User B adds a comment to the recipe.
  • Then: The system SHALL create a NEW_RECIPE_COMMENT notification for User A.

Validates

  • FR-28: Recipe Comments

docs.title: TS-39: Recipe Deletion RESTRICT
docs.text:
• Given: A recipe referenced in an active meal plan.
  • When: A user attempts to delete the recipe.
  • Then: The system SHALL return a validation error and prevent deletion.

Validates

  • FR-23: Recipe Management

docs.title: TS-40: Meal Time Configuration
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user.
  • When: The user creates "Lunch" with time 12:00 on weekdays and 13:00 on weekends.
  • Then: The system SHALL persist the meal time with 7 schedule entries.

Validates

  • FR-30: Meal Time Configuration

docs.title: TS-41: Meal Plan Creation (Week Boundary)
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user.
  • When: The user creates a meal plan for the week of Monday April 13, 2026.
  • Then: The system SHALL create a plan with week_start_date = 2026-04-13.

Validates

  • FR-31: Weekly Meal Plan

docs.title: TS-42: Duplicate Week Plan
docs.text:
• Given: A meal plan already exists for week of April 13, 2026.
  • When: A user attempts to create another plan for the same week.
  • Then: The system SHALL return a validation error due to the unique constraint on week_start_date.

Validates

  • FR-31: Weekly Meal Plan

docs.title: TS-43: Multi-Recipe Meal Assignment
docs.text:
• Given: A meal plan with a Monday dinner entry.
  • When: The user assigns "Grilled Chicken" and "Caesar Salad" to the entry for the entire household.
  • Then: The system SHALL create two meal_plan_entry_recipes records with null user_id.

Validates

  • FR-31: Weekly Meal Plan

docs.title: TS-44: Per-Member Assignment
docs.text:
• Given: A meal plan with a Monday breakfast entry.
  • When: The user assigns "Scrambled Eggs" to Jorge and "Toast" to Fernanda.
  • Then: The system SHALL create separate meal_plan_entry_recipes records with the respective user_id values.

Validates

  • FR-31: Weekly Meal Plan

docs.title: TS-45: Meal Plan Publish & Notify
docs.text:
• Given: A PENDING meal plan with entries.
  • When: The creator clicks "Notify Household".
  • Then: The system SHALL set status to PUBLISHED and create MEAL_PLAN_PUBLISHED notifications for all household members.

Validates

  • FR-32: Meal Plan Publish & Notify

docs.title: TS-48: Thumbs Up/Down on Meal
docs.text:
• Given: A meal entry marked as "done".
  • When: User A gives a thumbs-up.
  • Then: The system SHALL create a record in meal_plan_votes with vote = true for User A on that entry.
  • When: User A changes their vote to thumbs-down.
  • Then: The system SHALL update the existing vote record to vote = false.

Validates

  • FR-37: Meal Thumbs Up/Down

docs.title: TS-50: Meal Plan Retention
docs.text:
• Given: A meal plan with week_start_date 11 weeks ago.
  • When: The daily retention task executes at 03:00 AM.
  • Then: The system SHALL permanently delete the plan and all associated entries, recipes, and member records.

Validates

  • FR-34: Meal Plan Data Retention

docs.title: TS-51: Shopping List Export Preview
docs.text:
• Given: A meal plan with recipes requiring "Eggs" (6 UNIT) and "Milk" (1 L). An existing shopping list already has "Eggs" (3 UNIT).
  • When: The user requests an export preview targeting the existing list.
  • Then: The system SHALL return: existing items = [Eggs: 3→9 UNIT], new items = [Milk: 1 L].

Validates

  • FR-36: Shopping List Integration

docs.title: TS-52: Shopping List Export Confirm
docs.text:
• Given: A confirmed export preview.
  • When: The user confirms the export.
  • Then: The system SHALL increase "Eggs" quantity to 9 and add "Milk" (1 L) with no store assigned.

Validates

  • FR-36: Shopping List Integration

docs.title: TS-53: Mark Meal as Done
docs.text:
• Given: A meal plan entry that is not done.
  • When: A user marks it as done.
  • Then: The system SHALL set is_done = true.

Validates

  • FR-31: Weekly Meal Plan

docs.title: TS-54: Notification Delivery & Unread Count
docs.text:
• Given: User A has 3 unread notifications.
  • When: User A queries the unread count endpoint.
  • Then: The system SHALL return { count: 3 }.

Validates

  • FR-38: In-App Notifications

docs.title: TS-55: Mark Notification as Read
docs.text:
• Given: An unread notification for User A.
  • When: User A marks it as read.
  • Then: The system SHALL set read = true and the unread count SHALL decrease by 1.

Validates

  • FR-38: In-App Notifications

docs.title: TS-56: Send and Receive Message
docs.text:
• Given: User A and User B in the same household.
  • When: User A sends a message to User B.
  • Then: The system SHALL persist the message, create a NEW_MESSAGE notification for User B, and the message SHALL appear in both users' conversation view.

Validates

  • FR-39: User Messaging

docs.title: TS-57: Message Read Status
docs.text:
• Given: User B has an unread message from User A.
  • When: User B opens the conversation with User A.
  • Then: The system SHALL mark the message as read.

Validates

  • FR-39: User Messaging

docs.title: Summary & Environment
docs.text:
• Testcontainers: Used for PostgreSQL 17 integration.
  • Mocks: Google OAuth2 and People API are mocked for deterministic testing.
  • Verification: 100% of BDD scenarios (Happy & Sad) MUST pass.
  • Integrity: RFC 7807 validation for all error responses.
  • Immutability: email, firstName, and lastName remain unchanged after any profile update.

docs.title: TS-58: Centralized Photo Upload & Retrieval
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user.
  • When: The user uploads a photo via any module (profile, recipe, item, store).
  • Then: The system SHALL store the binary data in media.photos and return a URL reference (/api/images/{name}).
  • When: The URL is requested via GET.
  • Then: The system SHALL return the binary image with the correct Content-Type header.

Validates

  • FR-42: Centralized Media Service

docs.title: TS-59: Standalone Item Assignment to Meal Entry
docs.text:
• Given: A meal plan with a Monday breakfast entry.
  • When: The user assigns "Fresh Orange Juice" (2 UNIT) directly to the entry for the entire household.
  • Then: The system SHALL create a meal_plan_entry_items record with item_id, quantity=2, unit=UNIT, and user_id=null.

Validates

  • FR-41: Standalone Meal Items

docs.title: TS-60: Nutrient Master Catalog CRUD
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated Adult user.
  • When: The user creates a new nutrient "Omega-3" with unit "mg".
  • Then: The system SHALL persist the nutrient in recipes.nutrients.
  • When: The user attempts to delete a nutrient referenced by nutrition entries.
  • Then: The system SHALL return a validation error and prevent deletion.

Validates

  • FR-40: Nutrient Master Catalog

docs.title: TS-61: Ingredient Grouping
docs.text:
• Given: A recipe with ingredients.
  • When: The user adds "Flour" with group_name = "For the dough" and "Tomato Sauce" with group_name = "For the topping".
  • Then: The system SHALL persist the group_name and the UI SHALL display ingredients grouped by their group name.

Validates

  • FR-25: Recipe Ingredients

docs.title: TS-62: Coupon Barcode Rendering
docs.text:
• Given: A coupon with code = "SAVE10" and barcode_type = "QR".
  • When: The user views the coupon details.
  • Then: The system SHALL render a QR code encoding "SAVE10".

Validates

  • FR-13: Store Coupons

docs.title: TS-63: Shopping List Item Unavailability
docs.text:
• Given: A shopping list with an item "Organic Milk".
  • When: The user marks "Organic Milk" as unavailable.
  • Then: The system SHALL set unavailable = true and visually distinguish it from bought and pending items.

Validates

  • FR-10: In-Store Progress Tracking

docs.title: TS-64: Recipe Multiplier in Meal Plan
docs.text:
• Given: A meal plan entry with "Pancakes" assigned.
  • When: The user sets the multiplier to 2.0.
  • Then: The system SHALL persist multiplier = 2.0 on the meal_plan_entry_recipes record and use it for shopping list export quantity calculations.

Validates

  • FR-31: Weekly Meal Plan

docs.title: TS-65: Shopping Item Enriched Fields
docs.text:
• Given: An authenticated user creating a shopping item "Milk (1L Bottle)".
  • When: The user sets unit = "pcs", pc_quantity = 1.0, pc_unit = "L", nutrition_sample_size = 100, nutrition_sample_unit = "ml".
  • Then: The system SHALL persist all fields and use them for unit conversion and nutrition calculations.

Validates

  • FR-5: Category & Item Management

docs.title: TS-66: Meal Plan Export with Standalone Items
docs.text:
• Given: A meal plan with a recipe requiring "Eggs" (6 UNIT) and a standalone item "Orange Juice" (2 L).
  • When: The user requests an export preview.
  • Then: The system SHALL include both recipe ingredients and standalone items in the merge preview.

Validates

  • FR-36: Shopping List Integration
  • FR-41: Standalone Meal Items

docs.title: Requirements: Authentication & User Profiles
docs.text:
EARS Syntax Legend (Hover for trigger name)

  • Ubiquitous: "The system shall..." (Always true)
  • Event-driven: "When , the system shall..."
  • Unwanted Behavior: "If , then the system shall..."
  • State-driven: "While , the system shall..."
  • Optional: "Where , the system shall..."
  • Complex: Combinations of the above triggers.

docs.title: UJ-1: First-Time Login (Onboarding)
docs.text:
1. User navigates to the application and is presented with the "Login with Google" option.
  2. User clicks the button and is redirected to Google's identity provider.
  3. After successful authentication and consent, Google redirects the user back to the application.
  4. The system SHALL detect that this is a new user and automatically create a local User record and a default UserProfile.
  5. Age Group Bootstrapping: If this is the first user in the system, they SHALL be automatically assigned to the "Adult" age group, regardless of their actual age.
  6. Birthdate Synchronization: The system SHALL attempt to fetch the user's birthdate from Google via the People API.
  7. User is redirected to the Dashboard and sees their name and profile picture.

docs.title: UJ-2: Returning User Login
docs.text:
1. User navigates to the application.
  2. The system SHALL identify the existing session; if expired, the user clicks "Login with Google."
  3. Google authenticates the user (likely via an existing session).
  4. The system SHALL match the Google email with the existing local record and establish a session.
  5. User is redirected to the Dashboard.

docs.title: FR-1: Google OAuth2 Authentication
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. When the user initiates a login, the system shall redirect the user to Google's authorization provider.
  2. When Google returns a valid authorization code, the system shall exchange it for an access token and user identity information.
  3. If the Google authentication process fails or is cancelled, then the system shall redirect the user to the login page with a visible error parameter.

Rationale

So That I don't have to manage another set of credentials, I want to log in using my existing Google account.

docs.title: FR-2: Automatic User Registration
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. If an authenticated Google email does not exist in the local database, then the system shall create a new User record and a linked UserProfile.
  2. The system shall populate the new user's email, name, and profile picture URL using the data provided by the Google identity token.

Rationale

So That my experience is seamless, I want the system to automatically create an account for me when I first log in.

docs.title: FR-3: User Profile Quick View
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. While the user is authenticated, the system shall display a profile summary in the application header.
  2. Where a user has provided social links or a mobile phone number, the system shall display corresponding interactive icons in the quick view dropdown.

Rationale

So That I can quickly access my info, I want to see a summary in the header menu.

docs.title: FR-4: Update User Profile
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall allow users to modify their social links, mobile phone number, and profile photo.
  2. When a user updates their profile, the system shall ensure that exactly one family role is selected.
  3. The system shall prevent the modification of the email, firstName, and lastName fields.
  4. Where a user provides a local file, the system shall upload the file to the centralized media service and store the resulting URL reference as the profile photo.
  5. The system shall serve profile photos via the centralized media endpoint (/api/images/{name}), using URL-based references rather than inline Base64 data.

Rationale

So That I can customize my identity, I want to update my profile details.

docs.title: FR-16: Automated Age Group Classification
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall calculate the user's current age using their stored birthdate.
  2. The system shall map the user to an Age Group based on the current system-wide age range configurations.
  3. When a user logs in or the daily recalculation task runs, the system shall update the user's age group classification.

Rationale

So That the system can enforce age-appropriate access, users must be categorized into age groups.

docs.title: FR-17: Google Birthdate Integration
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall request the user.birthday.read scope during the initial OAuth2 authorization request.
  2. When a user completes their first login, the system shall attempt to retrieve their birthdate via the Google People API.
  3. If Google does not provide a birthdate, then the system shall prompt the user to provide it manually during the profile setup.

Rationale

So That onboarding is seamless, the system should automatically retrieve the user's birthday.

docs.title: NFR-1: Security (Zero Trust)
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. If a request is made to any /api/** endpoint without a valid session token, then the system shall return a 401 Unauthorized response.

docs.title: Requirements: Application Documentation
docs.text:
EARS Syntax Legend (Hover for trigger name)

  • Ubiquitous: "The system shall..." (Always true)
  • Event-driven: "When , the system shall..."
  • Unwanted Behavior: "If , then the system shall..."
  • State-driven: "While , the system shall..."
  • Optional: "Where , the system shall..."
  • Complex: Combinations of the above triggers.

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: The project SHALL maintain high-quality, professional documentation using MkDocs with the Material theme. This documentation serves as the central knowledge base for both developers (architecture, guidelines) and end-users (help, tutorials).

docs.title: FR-20: Integrated Documentation Build (MkDocs)
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall generate documentation using MkDocs.
  2. The system shall apply the Material for MkDocs theme to the generated site.
  3. The system shall include full-text search capability in the documentation site.
  4. The system shall render Mermaid diagrams embedded directly within the markdown source files.

Rationale

So That documentation remains consistent and reproducible, the system SHALL provide a dedicated build task for documentation generation.

docs.title: FR-21: Gradle Integration
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. When the documentation Gradle task is executed, the system shall trigger the MkDocs build process.
  2. The system shall isolate the documentation task from the main code compilation lifecycle.
  3. The system shall output the static HTML site to the standard build/docs/ directory.

Rationale

So That documentation can be managed alongside the code, the build process MUST be integrated into the Gradle lifecycle.

docs.title: FR-22: Content Organization
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall include a Technical Section containing architecture diagrams, design decisions (ADRs), and coding guidelines.
  2. The system shall include a Specification Section containing functional and non-functional requirements.
  3. The system shall include a User Section containing guides and help documentation for end-users.

Rationale

So That information is easily discoverable, the documentation site SHALL be organized into logical sections.

docs.title: NFR-5: Documentation Portability
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall generate the documentation as a collection of standalone static files (HTML/CSS/JS) capable of hosting on any standard web server.

docs.title: Requirements: Notifications & Messaging
docs.text:
EARS Syntax Legend (Hover for trigger name)

  • Ubiquitous: "The system shall..." (Always true)
  • Event-driven: "When , the system shall..."
  • Unwanted Behavior: "If , then the system shall..."
  • State-driven: "While , the system shall..."
  • Optional: "Where , the system shall..."
  • Complex: Combinations of the above triggers.

docs.title: FR-38: In-App Notifications
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall display a notification bell in the application header with an unread count badge.

  2. The system shall support the following notification types:

    Type Trigger MEAL_PLAN_PUBLISHED A user publishes a weekly meal plan MEAL_REMINDER Generated by the reminder scheduler before a meal's preparation time NEW_RECIPE_COMMENT A household member comments on a recipe NEW_MESSAGE A user sends a direct message

  3. The system shall store each notification with a type, title, message, link (URL path for navigation), sender_id, and sender_name.

  4. When a user clicks a notification, the system shall mark it as read and navigate to the URL specified in the link field.
  5. The system shall poll for new notifications via TanStack Query at a regular interval.
  6. When a user views the notifications list, the system shall display notifications sorted by created_at descending.

Rationale

So That I stay informed about household activity, I want to receive in-app notifications for important events.

docs.title: FR-39: User Messaging
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall allow any household member to send a direct message to another household member.
  2. The system shall track the read status of each message.
  3. When a message is sent, the system shall create a NEW_MESSAGE notification for the recipient.
  4. The system shall display messages grouped by conversation (per user pair), sorted by created_at ascending within a conversation.

Rationale

So That family members can communicate within the app, I want to send and receive messages.

docs.title: Requirements: Recipes & Meals Module
docs.text:
EARS Syntax Legend (Hover for trigger name)

  • Ubiquitous: "The system shall..." (Always true)
  • Event-driven: "When , the system shall..."
  • Unwanted Behavior: "If , then the system shall..."
  • State-driven: "While , the system shall..."
  • Optional: "Where , the system shall..."
  • Complex: Combinations of the above triggers.

docs.title: UJ-6: Recipe Creation
docs.text:
1. User A navigates to Recipes & Meals Recipes and clicks New Recipe.
  2. User A enters a name ("Grandma's Lasagna"), a markdown description, sets servings to 6, prep time to 90 minutes, and a source link.
  3. User A uploads two photos and selects one as the default photo for the recipes list.
  4. User A types labels: "Comfort Food" (autocompleted from existing) and "Italian" (created on demand).
  5. User A adds ingredients from the master item catalog: "Ground Beef" (500g), "Lasagna Sheets" (1 PACK), "Mozzarella" (200g).
  6. User A adds three preparation steps with markdown instructions and times, then reorders step 2 and 3 via drag-and-drop.
  7. User A saves the recipe. It appears in the recipes list with the default photo, name, average rating, and labels.

docs.title: UJ-7: Weekly Meal Planning
docs.text:
1. User A navigates to Recipes & Meals Meal Planner and creates a plan for the current week (Monday–Sunday).
  2. User A assigns "Scrambled Eggs" to Monday breakfast for Jorge and Vilma, and "Toast" for Fernanda.
  3. User A assigns "Grandma's Lasagna" + "Caesar Salad" (multi-recipe meal) to Monday dinner for the entire household.
  4. User A also assigns a standalone item "Fresh Orange Juice" (2 UNIT) to Monday breakfast for everyone.
  5. User A clicks Notify Household. All members receive an in-app notification with the plan status set to PUBLISHED.
  6. After dinner, Jorge marks Monday dinner as "done" and gives it a thumbs-up vote.

docs.title: UJ-8: Meal-to-Shopping-List Export
docs.text:
1. User A opens the current week's meal plan and clicks Export to Shopping List.
  2. The system generates a merge preview showing: new items to add and existing items whose quantities will increase.
  3. User A reviews the preview, selects an existing "Weekly Groceries" list as the target, and confirms.
  4. The system adds new ingredients without a store assignment and increases quantities for items already in the list.

docs.title: FR-23: Recipe Management
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall allow any household member to create, read, update, and delete recipes.
  2. The system shall store a recipe with a name, description (markdown), servings, source_link, video_link, and prep_time_minutes.
  3. The system shall support zero or more labels per recipe.
  4. When a user types a label that does not exist, the system shall create it automatically.
  5. When a user types a label, the system shall provide autocomplete suggestions from existing labels.
  6. When a label is removed from a recipe and no other recipe references it, the system shall delete the label from the database.
  7. If a recipe is referenced by any meal plan, then the system shall prevent its deletion and return a validation error.

Rationale

So That our family can build a shared cookbook, I want to create and manage recipes collaboratively.

docs.title: FR-24: Recipe Photos
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall allow users to upload one or more photos per recipe, stored via the centralized media service and referenced by photo_name.
  2. The system shall allow the user to designate one photo as the default photo (via an is_default boolean) for display in the recipes list.
  3. If no default photo is explicitly set and photos exist, then the system shall use the first uploaded photo as the default.

Rationale

So That recipes are visually appealing and easy to identify, I want to attach photos to my recipes.

docs.title: FR-25: Recipe Ingredients
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall allow users to add ingredients to a recipe by selecting items from the shopping.shopping_items master catalog.
  2. The system shall require a quantity and a unit (KG, G, L, ML, PACK, UNIT) for each ingredient, using the same unit enum as the Shopping module.
  3. The system shall allow a recipe to have one or more ingredients.
  4. Where a user specifies a group_name (e.g., "For the sauce", "For the dough"), the system shall group ingredients visually by that label.

Rationale

So That I know exactly what I need to prepare a recipe, I want to link catalog items as ingredients with precise quantities.

docs.title: FR-26: Nutrition Data
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall maintain a nutrients master catalog (recipes.nutrients) with predefined entries (e.g., Energy, Fat, Protein, Carbohydrate) each having a name, description, and unit.
  2. Where a shopping item has nutrition data, the system shall store zero or more nutrition_entries linking the item to a nutrient from the master catalog with a numeric value.
  3. The system shall define a nutrition_sample_size and nutrition_sample_unit per shopping item to specify the reference portion (e.g., "per 100g").
  4. When a recipe detail is requested, the system shall calculate the total nutrition values on-the-fly by summing each ingredient's nutrition entries (grouped by nutrient) scaled by its quantity relative to the sample size.
  5. If an ingredient has no nutrition entries, then the system shall exclude it from the nutrition totals and indicate which ingredients are missing data.

Rationale

So That I can track dietary intake with standardized nutrition metrics, I want a structured nutrition system backed by a managed nutrient catalog.

docs.title: FR-27: Preparation Steps
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall allow users to add one or more preparation steps to a recipe.
  2. The system shall store each step with an instruction (markdown) and an optional time_minutes.
  3. The system shall persist the order of steps via a sort_order column and present them in that order.
  4. The system shall support drag-and-drop reordering of steps in the UI.
  5. When a user reorders steps, the system shall update the sort_order values for all affected steps in a single transaction.

Rationale

So That anyone can follow the recipe, I want clear, ordered preparation instructions.

docs.title: FR-28: Recipe Comments
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall allow any household member to add comments to a recipe.
  2. While a comment exists, the system shall display the author's name and the timestamp.
  3. When a comment is added, the system shall create a NEW_RECIPE_COMMENT notification for the recipe creator.

Rationale

So That we can share tips and feedback, I want to comment on family recipes.

docs.title: FR-29: Recipe Ratings
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall allow each household member to rate a recipe from 1 to 5 stars.
  2. The system shall enforce a unique rating per user per recipe (updating replaces the previous rating).
  3. The system shall display the average rating on the recipe list and detail views.
  4. Where a user expands the rating section, the system shall display individual votes showing who rated, what score, and when.

Rationale

So That we can identify our favorite recipes, I want a collaborative rating system.

docs.title: FR-30: Meal Time Configuration
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall allow users to configure meal times (e.g., Breakfast, Lunch, Dinner, Snack).
  2. The system shall allow different times per day of the week for each meal time (e.g., Lunch at 12:00 on weekdays, 13:00 on weekends).
  3. The system shall store meal time schedules via a meal_time_schedules table linking meal times to day-of-week and time-of-day.

Rationale

So That the meal planner reflects our actual routine, I want to configure when we eat each meal.

docs.title: FR-31: Weekly Meal Plan
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall support weekly meal plans running Monday through Sunday (ISO 8601).
  2. The system shall enforce a unique week_start_date (the Monday) per meal plan.
  3. The system shall allow users to assign one or more recipes to a meal time entry (multi-recipe meals), each with an optional multiplier (default 1.0) for quantity scaling.
  4. The system shall allow users to assign standalone shopping items directly to a meal time entry (without requiring a recipe), specifying a quantity and unit.
  5. Where a recipe or item is assigned to specific members, the system shall record the user_id on the assignment. A null user_id means it applies to the entire household.
  6. When a user marks a meal entry as "done", the system shall update the is_done flag.
  7. The system shall track meal plan status using the values: PENDING, PUBLISHED, ACCEPTED, CHANGED.

Rationale

So That our family knows what to eat each day, I want to plan meals for the entire week.

docs.title: FR-32: Meal Plan Publish & Notify
docs.text:
Scope Change

The original approval workflow (per-member PENDING/ACCEPTED/CHANGED tracking) was dropped during implementation. Only the publish and notify flow remains.

Acceptance Criteria

  1. When a user clicks "Notify Household", the system shall change the meal plan status to PUBLISHED and create a MEAL_PLAN_PUBLISHED notification for all household members.

Rationale

So That everyone knows what's planned, I want to notify the household when the meal plan is ready.

docs.title: FR-33: Meal Preparation Reminders
docs.text:
Deferred

This feature is scaffolded but not yet functional. The MealReminderScheduler service exists but the reminder_offset_minutes column is not yet in the database schema.

Acceptance Criteria

  1. Where a meal plan entry has a reminder_offset_minutes configured, the system shall create a MEAL_REMINDER notification for all assigned members at the calculated time (meal time minus offset).
  2. The system shall check for pending reminders every 15 minutes via a scheduled task.

Rationale

So That I don't forget to start cooking, I want to receive a reminder before meal preparation time.

docs.title: FR-34: Meal Plan Data Retention
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall preserve the last 10 weeks of meal plan history.
  2. When the daily retention task executes (03:00 AM), the system shall permanently delete all meal plans with a week_start_date older than 10 weeks and their associated entries, recipes, and member records.

Rationale

So That the system remains performant, old meal plans should be removed automatically.

docs.title: FR-35: Meals This Week View
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall provide a dashboard widget summarizing the current week's planned meals.
  2. The system shall provide a dedicated page showing the full weekly meal plan with all entries, assigned recipes, and member statuses.
  3. The system shall determine the current week using the ISO 8601 Monday of the current date.

Rationale

So That I can quickly see what's planned, I want a "Meals This Week" view on the dashboard and as a full page.

docs.title: FR-36: Shopping List Integration
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. When a user requests an export, the system shall generate a merge preview listing new items and existing items with updated quantities.
  2. When the user confirms the preview, the system shall add ingredients to the selected shopping list or create a new one.
  3. The system shall use the same unit system (KG, G, L, ML, PACK, UNIT) as the Shopping module.
  4. If an ingredient item already exists in the target list, then the system shall increase the quantity by the recipe amount rather than creating a duplicate entry.
  5. The system shall NOT assign a store to newly added items unless the item already exists in the list with a store assignment.

Rationale

So That I can shop for what I need to cook, I want to export meal ingredients directly to my shopping list.

docs.title: FR-37: Meal Thumbs Up/Down
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. Where a meal entry is marked as "done", the system shall allow assigned members to give a thumbs-up or thumbs-down vote.
  2. The system shall store votes in a dedicated meal_plan_votes table with a unique constraint per entry and user (boolean: true = thumbs-up, false = thumbs-down).
  3. The system shall display aggregated vote counts (thumbs-up and thumbs-down totals) on each meal entry.

Rationale

So That we can track which meals the family enjoyed, I want to give quick feedback after eating.

docs.title: FR-40: Nutrient Master Catalog
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall maintain a recipes.nutrients master table with predefined nutrient entries (Energy, Fat, Saturated Fat, Mono-unsaturated Fat, Poly-unsaturated Fat, Carbohydrate, Sugars, Polyols, Starch, Fibre, Protein, Salt, Sodium, Vitamin A, Vitamin C, Calcium, Iron).
  2. The system shall store each nutrient with a name, optional description, and unit (e.g., kcal, g, mg, µg).
  3. While a user is classified as an Adult, the system shall allow the user to create, update, and delete nutrients via the Nutrient Settings page (/settings/nutrients).
  4. If a nutrient is referenced by any nutrition entry, then the system shall prevent its deletion.

Rationale

So That nutrition tracking uses standardized, consistent metrics, I want a managed catalog of nutrients rather than free-form text entries.

docs.title: FR-41: Standalone Meal Items
docs.text:
Acceptance Criteria

  1. The system shall allow users to assign shopping items directly to a meal plan entry without requiring a recipe.
  2. The system shall store each meal item assignment with a reference to the shopping item, a quantity, a unit, and an optional user_id for per-member assignment.
  3. The system shall include standalone meal items in the shopping list export alongside recipe ingredients.

Rationale

So That I can plan simple items (fruit, yogurt, juice) without creating a recipe for each, I want to assign items directly to meals.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
The Home Application is a centralized platform designed to help families collaborate on household activities. This index provides a complete map of all user journeys, functional requirements, non-functional requirements, and architectural decisions.

  • The North Star

    To provide a unified, secure entry point for household management, establishing a trusted environment for sharing resources.

  • Stakeholders

    • Home Users: Collaborative family members.
    • Developers: Building with modular APIs.
    • Admins: Ensuring data integrity and health.

docs.title: Requirement Classifications
docs.text:
To ensure clarity and traceability, all specifications are categorized into four distinct types:

  • User Journeys (UJ)

    Narrative-driven scenarios that describe a specific end-to-end goal from the perspective of a user.

  • Functional Requirements (FR)

    Specific features, behaviors, or tasks the system MUST perform to satisfy user needs.

  • Non-Functional Requirements (NFR)

    Quality attributes such as performance, security, and reliability that define how the system operates.

  • Architectural Decisions (AD/ADR)

    Fundamental design choices and constraints that shape the system's structure and technical direction.

docs.title: EARS Pattern
docs.text:
The Home Application project utilizes the EARS (Easy Approach to Requirements Syntax) pattern to ensure that all requirements are clear, concise, and verifiable.

EARS Syntax Legend (Hover for trigger name)

  • Ubiquitous: "The system shall..." (Always true)
  • Event-driven: "When , the system shall..."
  • Unwanted Behavior: "If , then the system shall..."
  • State-driven: "While , the system shall..."
  • Optional: "Where , the system shall..."
  • Complex: Combinations of the above triggers.

    • EARS Documentation

docs.title: 1. User Journeys
docs.text: ID Journey Description UJ-1  Onboarding First-time Google login and profile setup. UJ-2  Returning User Seamless session restoration for existing users. UJ-3  Collaborative Shopping Multiple users updating a shared list in real-time. UJ-4  Checkout Optimization Using digital cards and coupons at the store. UJ-5  Family Setup Configuring age ranges and household roles. UJ-6  Recipe Creation Creating a recipe with photos, ingredients, and steps. UJ-7  Weekly Meal Planning Planning meals for the week and notifying the household. UJ-8  Meal-to-Shopping-List Exporting meal ingredients to a shopping list.
